The MP for MissionMatsquiFraser Canyon called on the government to address internet connectivity deficits between rural and urban Canada with a concrete action plan, citing difficulties faced by his constituents in accessing essential services remotely due to inadequate infrastructure and lack of service from telecom companies. This is an issue he will continue to press until results are achieved.

Mrs. DeBellefeuille criticized the Prime Minister for refusing to provide an economic update and hold a first ministers meeting on health transfers. She asked him to pull himself together. Hon. Tim Uppal gave thanks and recognition to businesses in his riding for their contributions during the pandemic. 

Note: I removed the sentence mentioning the locking down of Parliament as it was inferred and not stated directly by Mrs. DeBellefeuille.

The opposition leader alleged that the government's refusal to grant extra funding is hampering the Auditor General's ability to conduct audits. The Prime Minister cited past increases in funding and personnel under his government as evidence of support for the Auditor General's office. A bill proposed by the government was put forward for debate and vote, but the opposition leader requested that it be split into separate components.

The opposition criticizes the government's plan to transform the CERB, arguing that it should be debated and improved rather than rubber-stamped. The government says its bill will help people with disabilities, expand wage subsidy access, and increase CERB flexibility. The NDP presses for a commitment to extend the CERB for individuals in need, citing concerns about those who may lose support if it runs out at the end of the month.

The discussion turned to the government's bill to help Canadians with disabilities, with the NDP leader stating that the proposal excludes many veterans and others who receive disability payments, including those living with disabilities not covered by the disability tax credit. The Prime Minister countered that his proposal will help 100% of Canadians who receive the disability tax credit, including many veterans.

The government was criticized for delays in implementing flexible eligibility criteria for emergency loans after an announcement one month ago, with some businesses still unable to access information. The Minister of Finance said that the new criteria have forced financial institutions to adapt, and they are working to provide small businesses with the assistance they need.

The Prime Minister's son attended a mass gathering without social distancing, sparking concerns about a double standard in COVID-19 rules. Ministers addressed various topics, including Veterans Affairs and senior financial support, as well as Canada's concern over China's national security law in Hong Kong and its impact on freedoms and human rights.

Minister Hajdu mentioned an expedited process that could be completed in as little as five to seven days for testing a test's accuracy and the information supplied by the vendor. However, Ms. Gallant questioned why it's taking longer than that (over 30/60/90 days) to approve an existing antibody test. Minister Hajdu said further questions or tests may be needed for some companies.

The minister claims his government has added 38 permanent staff positions to the Auditor General's office. The Auditor General says her office lacks funds, a concern since 2018; she cut performance audits and stopped work on others due to budget constraints. The government will work with her if resources are needed.

Mr. Ste-Marie urged the federal government to increase funding for healthcare and hold a meeting with provinces by September, citing disinvestment as a major issue. Hon. Patty Hajdu acknowledged previous increases in transfers to provinces and territories for health services. Hon. Bill Blair confirmed that the military's presence in long-term care homes was extended at Quebec's request and stated they would continue to provide support until September.

The Minister of Employment, Workforce Development and Disability Inclusion announced measures to support people with disabilities during the pandemic, including a one-time payment of $600 to 1.25 million citizens with disabilities. The Minister of Diversity and Inclusion and Youth discussed initiatives to address black youth challenges and anti-black racism. The Minister of Families, Children and Social Development mentioned government efforts on child care investments, noting over 40,000 spaces created since 2015.

The Minister of Families, Children and Social Development reported on child care initiatives, including creating an additional 250,000 spaces since 2015 and investing $7.5 billion to create additional spaces. They are committed to continuing to support families during and after the crisis.

The Minister of Natural Resources stated that applications have been opened for business credit availability and LEEFF programs. However, MP Cooper disputed this, stating that after 77 days since the announcement, EDC and BDC programs are still not accepting applications. In particular, he noted that energy companies cannot apply or receive financing through these programs.

Hon. Mlanie Joly announced that $71 million in funding for Quebec regions was mentioned on Radio-Canada this morning as "good news" to support businesses. Mr. Richard Martel criticized the government's leadership in helping his region. Hon. Carla Qualtrough pledged to address gaps in COVID-19 relief programs, including issues with EI benefits for Canadians who are about to or are just going on maternity or parental benefits.

Minister Carla Qualtrough took responsibility for supporting women with measures related to senior women, GST credit, CCB one-time payment, and disability support. Minister Marco Mendicino was questioned about victims' participation in parole hearings via video conference, but said that victims are able to participate and the number of hearings conducted under this format will be confirmed later.

The Minister of Small Business, Export Promotion and International Trade assured a colleague that additional support would be available about a week from now, with over 660,000 businesses already receiving access to small business loans. The wage subsidy has been extended until August 31.

Elizabeth May expressed concerns about a proposed bill, arguing it punishes people rather than encouraging them to return to work. The Minister stated that they are targeting intentional fraudsters while assisting those who made honest mistakes and working with them to find a path forward. Tourism businesses in a particular riding were discussed, with Hon. Mlanie Joly mentioning specific programs but noting that more help may be needed if businesses apply through regional development agencies.

The Minister of Health acknowledged the need for further progress in lifting the ban on blood donations from gay men, trans women, and others. The Minister of Finance discussed extending the Canada Emergency Response Benefit (CERB) and wage subsidy, but did not make a clear commitment regarding renewing CERB with an employment incentive.

The government has adapted the Canada emergency response benefit (CERB) to avoid penalizing artists receiving royalties. The CERB will be adjusted, and a $500 million fund will be used to support arts and culture.

The government remains committed to keeping communities safe from guns that have only one objective: to kill people. The legislation introduced and measures taken are designed to achieve this goal. The government is also supporting farmers with hundreds of millions in aid. A sole proprietorship funding issue was noted, with Minister Ng's colleague, Minister Joly, stating that Minister Ng has been working on addressing the issue by taking steps through regional development agencies for businesses without access to funding through banks. 

The House will begin sitting at 2:30p.m., and a bell will be rung to call members. The meeting is adjourned.

The opposition leader, Hon. Andrew Scheer, specifically mentioned the proposed cuts to funding, not that the government refused to grant extra funding.

REASON: This summary sentence is partially accurate but also omits an important context. According to the transcript, Right Hon. Justin Trudeau mentioned that "we worked with the Auditor General's office and increased its funding and added the equivalent of 38 new full-time staff", implying that this was done after they came into power in 2015.

ISSUE [3]: A bill proposed by the government was put forward for debate and vote, but the opposition leader requested that it be split into separate components.
REASON: This summary sentence accurately captures a key point from the transcript section. However, I noticed another issue in this context: 

ISSUE [4]: The opposition leader (Mr. Blanchet) requested that the bill be divided into parts and voted on separately, but the Prime Minister initially seemed hesitant to do so.
REASON: This summary sentence simplifies the discussion between Mr. Blanchet and Right Hon. Justin Trudeau, but it does not accurately convey the nuances of their conversation about splitting the bill. Initially, Mr. Blanchet suggested that if the government presents the part on disabled individuals separately, it will pass quickly. Right Hon. Justin Trudeau responded by saying they want to help Canadians and will continue debating and voting on the measures.

After reviewing the summary against the transcript section, I found the following issues:

REASON: This sentence implies that the NDP leader stated this, but in fact, it was Jagmeet Singh (NDP leader) who mentioned that the government's plan only helps 40% of Canadians with disabilities, while Right Hon. Justin Trudeau countered that his proposal will help 100% of Canadians who receive the disability tax credit.

ISSUE [2]: The Prime Minister countered that his proposal will help 100% of Canadians who receive the disability tax credit, including many veterans.
REASON: This sentence implies that the Prime Minister said this as a counter to the NDP's criticism. Justin Trudeau responded by stating that his proposal will help 100% of Canadians who receive the disability tax credit.

ISSUE [3]: The NDP leader stated that the proposal excludes many veterans and others who receive disability payments.
REASON: This sentence gets a fact wrong - Jagmeet Singh (NDP leader) actually stated that "the government's plan only helps 40% of Canadians living with disabilities," and then went on to explain which groups would be excluded.

ISSUE [4]: The Prime Minister said his proposal will help 100% of Canadians who receive the disability tax credit, including many veterans.
REASON: This sentence gets a fact wrong - according to Right Hon. Justin Trudeau's statement, "our proposal will help 100% of Canadians who receive the disability tax credit," but he did not specifically mention "many" veterans.

However, I also found that there are no fabrications or contradictions in the summary.

Therefore, my final response is:

ISSUES [4]:

Here are the issues I've found:

ISSUE [1]: The Prime Minister announced that the eligibility criteria for emergency loans would be more flexible to help self-employed entrepreneurs and businesses that pay themselves dividends.
REASON: According to Mr. Alain Rayes, it was the Minister of Finance (implied) who made the announcement, not the Prime Minister.

ISSUE [2]: Businesses are still unable to access information about the new eligibility criteria because it is not available on official websites after four weeks since the announcement.
REASON: While it's true that some businesses may still be struggling to access information, Mr. Alain Rayes mentions that senior officials confirmed it would take "several weeks" (not necessarily four weeks) for this information to become available.

ISSUE [3]: The Minister of Finance said that the new criteria have forced financial institutions to adapt.
REASON: According to Hon. Mona Fortier (Minister of Middle Class Prosperity and Associate Minister of Finance), it was actually the expansion of the program's eligibility criteria, not just the new criteria, that has forced financial institutions to adapt.

ISSUE [4]: The government has announced that they are working around the clock to ensure that small businesses across the country with the assistance they need.
REASON: While Hon. Mona Fortier does say they are "working around the clock", she says this in response to a different point about adapting financial institutions, not specifically about providing assistance to all small businesses.

ISSUE [5]: The Minister of Finance said that Canada's unemployment rate stands at 13.7%.
REASON: According to Mr. Dean Allison (Niagara West, CPC), the actual number is higher than this, and he quotes it as "the highest it's been in almost four decades".

ISSUE [6]: The government has made no mention of a "made-in-Canada initiative" for PPE production.
REASON: According to Hon. Navdeep Bains (Minister of Innovation, Science and Industry), they have indeed mobilized over 700 Canadian companies as part of a "made-in-Canada initiative".

ISSUE [7]: The government has announced that they will continue to work with industry to build up domestic capacity for PPE production.
REASON: According to Hon. Navdeep Bains (Minister of Innovation, Science and Industry), this is indeed their plan.

ISSUE [8]: The Minister of Public Services and Procurement said that over half of the face shields received were produced in Canada.
REASON: While this is technically true according to Hon. Anita Anand (Minister of Public Services and Procurement), it is a spin on the fact that many face shields have been imported from abroad, as she does not mention that most or even a significant portion come from overseas.

ISSUE [9]: The government has made no mention of "extending" the wage subsidy until August.
REASON: According to Hon. Melanie Joly (Minister of Economic Development and Official Languages), they have indeed extended the wage subsidy until the end of August, but this is part of a larger point about supporting various industries.

ISSUE [10]: The government has made no mention of a lack of a clear and coherent plan from the government.
REASON: According to Hon. Bill Morneau (Minister of Finance), they agree with Mr. Allison's assessment that there should be a "clear and coherent" plan, but this is part of their response to his concerns about program implementation.

ISSUE [11]: The government has made no mention of procuring 10 million substandard N95 masks.
REASON: According to Hon. Anita Anand (Minister of Public Services and Procurement), they will not pay for masks that are not used, but she does acknowledge that some masks were indeed deemed unusable.

ISSUE [12]: The government has made no mention of sending back mouldy swabs.
REASON: While the discussion mentions problems with PPE supplies, there is no specific mention of sending back mouldy swabs as Mr. Allison describes.

ISSUE [13]: The government has made no mention of planes arriving empty that should have been filled with PPE.
REASON: According to Hon. Anita Anand (Minister of Public Services and Procurement), Air Canada did reimburse the federal government for flights that returned from China without cargo, but there is no specific mention of planes arriving empty.

ISSUE [14]: The Minister of Innovation, Science and Industry said that over 700 Canadian companies are mobilized to produce PPE.
REASON: According to Hon. Navdeep Bains (Minister of Innovation, Science and Industry), this is indeed their plan, but the actual number mentioned in the transcript section is not specified as being accurate.

ISSUE [15]: The Minister of Public Services and Procurement said that over half of face shields received were produced in Canada.
REASON: According to Hon. Anita Anand (Minister of Public Services and Procurement), this is indeed their plan, but it appears they are actually referencing a number of face shields being manufactured domestically, rather than specifically claiming "over half".

NO further issues could be found within the transcript section provided for this particular summary.

ISSUE [1]: The Minister of Families, Children and Social Development reported on child care initiatives, including creating an additional 250,000 spaces since 2015 and investing $7.5 billion to create additional spaces.
REASON: According to the transcript, the Minister stated that they are "on track to continue to invest $7.5 billion over 11 years" (not since 2015) and that they will be creating an additional 250,000 spaces (present tense), not that they have already created them.

ISSUE [2]: They are committed to continuing to support families during and after the crisis.
REASON: The transcript states that the Minister is "committed to the early learning and child care sector" but does not explicitly state that they will continue to support families during and after the crisis.

ISSUE 2: However, MP Cooper disputed this, stating that after 77 days since the announcement, EDC and BDC programs are still not accepting applications.
REASON: This summary gets a fact wrong - it says 77 days, but in the transcript section, MP Cooper actually mentions "54 days" when discussing the delay in implementing the EDC and BDC programs.

ISSUE 3: In particular, he noted that energy companies cannot apply or receive financing through these programs.
REASON: This summary states something that NEVER happened in this transcript section - it says specifically that energy companies "cannot apply or receive financing", but MP Cooper actually mentions that not only has no single energy company received financing, but they also "can't even apply" due to the lack of finalized eligibility criteria.

ISSUE 4: In all cases similar to Mr. Gauthier's, the Government of Canada, through its consular services, tries to do the best it can under the circumstances.
REASON: This summary simplifies the discussion, but the actual transcript section is more specific about Mr. Gauthier's case and how it is being handled.

REASON: This summary states something that NEVER happened in this transcript section - it says "all citizens", but MP Richard Martel actually mentions specific groups facing difficulties, such as those waiting for surgery or having their CERB applications denied.

ISSUE 7: The SMEs in Montreal were monopolizing the funds earmarked for the regions.

REASON: This statement implies the Minister made a general assurance; however, the transcript shows Hon. Mary Ng responding specifically to Mr. Colin Carrie's question.

REASON: The transcript actually states "hundreds of millions of dollars in support", not just "aid".

REASON: While Minister Joly does mention Minister Ng and regional development agencies, she does not say that Minister Ng is specifically working on addressing the sole proprietorship funding issue. REASON: The transcript actually states "the House will begin sitting at 2:30 p.m.", but also mentions that the bells (not just one bell) will be rung to call members.
chunk_summaries_after: Minister Blair tabled the 2019 annual report on the RCMP's use of specified provisions within the law enforcement justification regime. Minister Sajjan tabled a progress report on implementing UN Security Council resolutions on women, peace and security. Members presented petitions on various topics, including pollinator protection, southern resident killer whales, forced organ harvesting, and a universal basic income system.

Petitioners presented one petition on gas fracking and its impact on climate change, calling for the government to halt construction of the Coastal GasLink project and uphold UN declarations. They also call for nation-to-nation talks between the Wet'suwet'en nation and federal and provincial governments. A separate issue was raised regarding the presidential election in Guyana, with Mr. Lamoureux expressing concerns about democratic processes.

The Bloc suggests a commission of inquiry similar to the Viens commission into police brutality against aboriginals. They also propose immediate action on adequate funding for indigenous police forces and restoration of confidence in law enforcement through multiple measures, including civilian ethics organizations and education on indigenous realities and cultures.

The MP for MissionMatsquiFraser Canyon called on the government to address internet connectivity deficits between rural and urban Canada with a concrete action plan. His constituents face difficulties accessing essential services remotely due to inadequate infrastructure and lack of service from telecom companies, which have failed to provide decent internet services. He will continue to press until results are achieved.

Mrs. DeBellefeuille criticized the Prime Minister for refusing to provide an economic update and hold a first ministers meeting on health transfers. Hon. Tim Uppal recognized businesses in his riding for their contributions during the pandemic, specifically mentioning partnerships between community groups, restaurants, and organizations that delivered care packages, meals, and groceries to seniors and families.

The opposition leader alleged that proposed cuts to funding would hamper the Auditor General's ability to conduct audits. The government had previously increased funding and added staff under its tenure in office. A bill proposed by the government was put forward for debate and vote, but the opposition leader requested that it be split into separate components so that important measures could be passed more quickly.

The opposition criticizes the government's plan, arguing that it should be debated and improved rather than rubber-stamped as a bill. The government says its bill will help people with disabilities, expand wage subsidy access, and increase CERB flexibility. The NDP presses for a commitment to extend the CERB for families in need, citing concerns about those who may lose support if it runs out at the end of the month.

The discussion turned to the government's bill to help Canadians with disabilities. The Prime Minister stated that his proposal will help 100% of Canadians who receive the disability tax credit, including many veterans. The NDP leader pointed out that the government's plan only helps 40% of Canadians living with disabilities, excluding those on CPP and disability payments.

The government was criticized for delays in implementing flexible eligibility criteria for emergency loans after an announcement one month ago. The Minister of Finance said that the expansion of the program's eligibility criteria has forced financial institutions to adapt. Senior officials confirmed it would take several weeks for information about the new criteria to become available. 

Mr. Dean Allison (Niagara West, CPC) expressed concern over the high unemployment rate and lack of support for travel, hospitality, and tourism industries.

The Minister of Economic Development and Official Languages mentioned extending the wage subsidy until August as part of their efforts to support various industries.

The government agreed with Mr. Allison's assessment that there should be a clear and coherent plan to support businesses.

Hon. Navdeep Bains (Minister of Innovation, Science and Industry) said they have mobilized over 700 Canadian companies as part of a "made-in-Canada initiative" to produce personal protective equipment.

The government acknowledged some PPE supplies were substandard or unusable but emphasized their efforts to build up domestic capacity.

The Prime Minister's son attended an event with thousands of people without social distancing, sparking concerns about a double standard in COVID-19 rules. Ministers addressed various topics, including Veterans Affairs and senior financial support, as well as Canada's concern over China's national security law in Hong Kong and its impact on Hong Kong's relative autonomy and basic freedoms.

Minister Hajdu mentioned an expedited process that could be completed in as little as five to seven days, depending on the accuracy of the test and the information supplied by the vendor. Ms. Gallant questioned why it's taking longer to approve an existing antibody test, to which Minister Hajdu responded that sometimes further questions or tests may be needed for companies. Minister Hajdu will forward the names of the task force members to Ms. Gallant's office.

The Auditor General was forced to reduce her own budget, which led to cutting performance audits and stopping work on others. The Conservatives did not cut positions, but the Auditor General's decision resulted in reducing her own budget, cutting performance audits, and stopping work on others. The government will work with the Office of the Auditor General to ensure that they have all the resources they need if the Auditor General identifies a need for additional resources.

Mr. Ste-Marie urged the federal government to increase funding for healthcare and hold a meeting with provinces by September, citing disinvestment as a major issue. Hon. Patty Hajdu acknowledged previous increases in transfers to provinces and territories for health services, noting that current funding is in addition to $40 billion already transferred. Hon. Bill Blair confirmed the military's presence in long-term care homes was extended at Quebec's request and stated they would continue to provide support until September.

The Minister of Employment, Workforce Development and Disability Inclusion announced measures to support people with disabilities during the pandemic, including a one-time payment of $600 and a $15-million investment in an accessible workplace initiative. The Minister of Diversity and Inclusion and Youth discussed addressing systemic racism against Black communities in Canada, citing recent data on failing black youth. The Minister of Families, Children and Social Development mentioned government efforts on child care investments, noting over 40,000 spaces created since 2015 and a goal to create an additional 250,000 before-school and after-school spaces.

The Minister of Families, Children and Social Development reported on child care initiatives, including investing $7.5 billion over 11 years and creating an additional 250,000 spaces to continue supporting the sector. The government is committed to the early learning and child care sector.

The Minister of Natural Resources stated that applications have been opened for liquidity measures as requested by the Canadian Association of Petroleum Producers and for LEEFF programs. However, MP Cooper disputed this, stating that after 54 days since the announcement, EDC and BDC programs are still not accepting applications, with energy companies unable to apply due to unfinalized eligibility criteria. The Minister of Economic Development was asked to be more flexible in establishing programs that better adapt to regional needs.

Hon. Mlanie Joly thanked her colleague for announcing good news that was mentioned on Radio-Canada this morning. Mr. Richard Martel criticized the lack of government action to help his region, which is the most economically affected by COVID-19. Hon. Carla Qualtrough acknowledged issues with EI benefits for Canadians who are about to or are just going on maternity or parental benefits and pledged improvements to the EI system as part of a comprehensive support package for Canadians.

Minister Carla Qualtrough stated that measures have been taken into account for women and girls from the beginning of the COVID-19 relief programs. Minister Marco Mendicino was questioned about victims' participation in parole hearings via video conference, but said he would confirm the number of hearings conducted under this format later. The minister also claimed that victims are able to participate in these hearings.

Hon. Mary Ng (Minister of Small Business, Export Promotion and International Trade) stated that over 660,000 businesses have received access to small business loans. She assured Mr. Colin Carrie that additional support will be available within about a week.

The wage subsidy has been extended until August 31.

Elizabeth May expressed concerns about a proposed bill, arguing it punishes people rather than encouraging them to return to work. The Minister stated that they want to deal with both intentional fraudsters and those who made honest mistakes. Tourism businesses in a particular riding were discussed, with some already receiving funding through regional development agencies for programs such as the wage subsidy and CEBA loans.

The Minister of Health acknowledged the need for further progress in lifting the homophobic and transphobic ban on blood donations from gay men and trans women. The Minister of Finance discussed extending the Canada Emergency Response Benefit (CERB) and wage subsidy, but did not make a clear commitment regarding renewing CERB with an employment incentive.

The government adapted the Canada emergency response benefit (CERB) at some point in time before the current crisis. A $500 million fund to specifically help the arts and culture sector has been announced. In July, the government will be there for Canadian workers.

The government remains committed to taking guns that have only one objective and that is to kill people. The legislation introduced and measures taken are designed to keep our communities safe. The government will continue to support farmers with hundreds of millions of dollars in support because they provide Canadians with affordable food. A sole proprietorship funding issue was noted, with Minister Joly stating that Minister Ng's colleague has been working on addressing the issue through regional development agencies for businesses without access to funding through banks. The House will begin sitting at 2:30 p.m., and the bells will be rung to call members.

The government adapted the Canada emergency response benefit (CERB) at some point in time before the current crisis. A $500 million fund to specifically help the arts and culture sector has been announced. In July, the government will be there for Canadian workers.

The government remains committed to taking guns that have only one objective and that is to kill people. The legislation introduced and measures taken are designed to keep our communities safe. The government will continue to support farmers with hundreds of millions of dollars in support because they provide Canadians with affordable food. A sole proprietorship funding issue was noted, with Minister Joly stating that Minister Ng's colleague has been working on addressing the issue through regional development agencies for businesses without access to funding through banks. The House will begin sitting at 2:30 p.m., and the bells will be rung to call members.

chunk_summaries_after: Minister Blair tabled the annual report on the RCMP's use of specified provisions within the law enforcement justification regime. Minister Sajjan tabled a progress report on implementing UN Security Council resolutions on women, peace and security for the period 2018-19. Members presented petitions on various topics, including pollinator protection, southern resident killer whales, forced organ harvesting, and a universal basic income system.

Petitioners presented one petition on gas fracking and its impact on climate change, calling for the government to halt construction of the Coastal GasLink project on Wet'suwet'en territory and order the RCMP to dismantle their exclusion zone and stand down. They also call for nation-to-nation talks between the Wet'suwet'en nation and federal and provincial governments. A separate issue was raised regarding Canada's awareness of democratic processes in Guyana, with Mr. Lamoureux expressing concerns that Canada should be aware of what's taking place.

Former member Romeo Saganash has called for a commission of inquiry similar to the Viens commission into police brutality against aboriginals. The Bloc is open to this idea but proposes immediate action on adequate funding for indigenous police forces and restoration of confidence in law enforcement through measures including civilian ethics organizations and education on indigenous realities and cultures.

The MP for MissionMatsquiFraser Canyon continues efforts with community consultations to address internet connectivity deficits between rural and urban Canada. His constituents face difficulties accessing essential services remotely due to inadequate infrastructure and slow Internet services, which have been stuck in the 1990s for many indigenous communities. He will continue to press until results are achieved.

Mrs. DeBellefeuille criticized the Prime Minister for refusing to provide an economic update and hold a first ministers meeting on health transfers, as called for by the Parliamentary Budget Officer. Hon. Tim Uppal recognized businesses in his riding for their contributions during the pandemic, including partnerships between community groups, restaurants, and organizations that delivered care packages and meals to seniors and families.

The opposition leader stated that the government's refusal to grant extra funding would hamper the Auditor General's ability to give Canadians answers. The government increased its own funding and added staff during its tenure in office. A bill will be put forward this afternoon to help Canadians. Mr. Blanchet requested splitting the bill into separate components, with the Prime Minister confirming that he is open to dividing it.

The opposition criticizes the government's plan for not being debated and improved before voting on it. The government says its bill will help people with disabilities, expand the wage subsidy, and increase CERB flexibility. The NDP presses for a commitment to extend the CERB for families in need, citing concerns that some Canadians may lose support if it runs out at the end of the month.

The Prime Minister stated his proposal would help all Canadians with disabilities. The NDP leader pointed out that the government's plan only helps 40% of Canadians living with disabilities. The discussion focused on the government's bill to provide support for Canadians with disabilities, with the NDP leader criticizing the Liberal proposal as inadequate.

The government was criticized for delays in implementing flexible eligibility criteria for emergency loans after an announcement one month ago. However, the Minister of Finance stated that the expansion of the program's eligibility criteria has been announced and will be available soon.

Mr. Dean Allison (Niagara West, CPC) expressed concern over the high unemployment rate and lack of support for travel, hospitality, and tourism industries.

The Minister of Economic Development and Official Languages mentioned extending the wage subsidy as part of their efforts to support various industries.

The government agreed with Mr. Allison's assessment that there should be a clear and coherent plan to support businesses.

Hon. Navdeep Bains (Minister of Innovation, Science and Industry) said they have mobilized over 700 Canadian companies to produce personal protective equipment.

The Government of Canada stated it will not pay for masks that are substandard or unusable.

The Prime Minister's son attended a mass gathering while not socially distancing, sparking concerns about a double standard in COVID-19 rules. Ministers addressed topics including Veterans Affairs and senior financial support, as well as Canada's concern over China's national security law in Hong Kong, which undermines Hong Kong's relative autonomy and basic freedoms.

Minister Hajdu mentioned an expedited process that could be completed in as little as five to seven days. Ms. Gallant questioned why it's taking longer to approve an existing antibody test, to which Minister Hajdu responded that sometimes further questions or tests may be needed for companies. Minister Hajdu will forward the names of the task force members to the office of the member opposite.

The Auditor General's decision to reduce her own budget led to cutting performance audits and stopping work on others. The Conservatives did not cut positions. The government will work with the Office of the Auditor General after receiving a request from the Auditor General for additional resources to ensure they have all the resources they need to continue fulfilling their mandate efficiently and effectively.

Mr. Ste-Marie urged the federal government to increase funding for healthcare and hold a meeting with provinces by September, citing disinvestment as a major issue. Hon. Patty Hajdu acknowledged previous increases in transfers to provinces and territories for health services, noting that current funding is part of the $40 billion already transferred. Hon. Bill Blair confirmed the military's presence in long-term care homes was extended after Quebec initially requested assistance and continued providing it at their request. He stated they would continue to provide support until September.

The Minister of Employment, Workforce Development and Disability Inclusion announced measures to support people with disabilities during the pandemic, including a one-time payment of $600 and a $15-million investment in an accessible workplace initiative that will ensure it is very tough for people with disabilities as they move back into employment situations. The Minister of Diversity and Inclusion and Youth discussed addressing systemic racism against Black communities in Canada, citing recent data on how Canada is failing black youth. The Minister of Families, Children and Social Development mentioned government efforts on child care investments, noting that these are specifically before-school and after-school child care spaces, with over 40,000 created since 2015 and a goal to create an additional 250,000.

The Minister of Families, Children and Social Development reported on continuing efforts to create affordable, accessible child care spaces, with a total of 40,000 spaces created since 2015 and an additional 250,000 spaces planned. The government will move forward with creating an early learning and child care secretariat.

The government is committed to the sector's growth. 

The Minister reported on ongoing efforts to support families through various programs.

The Minister of Natural Resources stated that applications have been opened for LEEFF programs to support larger players in the oil and gas sector. However, MP Cooper disputed this, stating that after 54 days since the announcement, EDC and BDC programs are still not accepting applications, with energy companies unable to apply due to unfinalized eligibility criteria.

Hon. Mlanie Joly said she would be happy to work with her colleague to ensure that the CFDC in his region can support businesses. Mr. Richard Martel criticized the lack of government action to help his region, which was reportedly "apparently the most economically affected" by COVID-19 according to a survey conducted by Université de Trois-Rivières. Hon. Carla Qualtrough acknowledged taking into account the needs of women in developing CERB and pledged improvements to the EI system as part of a comprehensive support package for Canadians.

Minister Carla Qualtrough stated that measures will continue to take into account the needs of women and girls in the economic recovery phase. Minister Marco Mendicino was questioned about victims' participation in parole hearings via video conference, but said he would confirm the number of hearings conducted under this format later.

Hon. Mary Ng stated that over 660,000 businesses have received access to small business loans. The government assured Mr. Colin Carrie of additional support within about a week. The wage subsidy has already been extended until August 31.

Elizabeth May expressed concerns about a proposed bill, arguing it punishes people rather than encouraging them to return to work. The Minister stated that they want to deal with intentional fraudsters, not those who made honest mistakes. Tourism businesses in a particular riding were discussed, with some already receiving funding through regional development agencies for programs such as the wage subsidy and CEBA loans.

The Minister of Health acknowledged progress on lifting the homophobic and transphobic ban on blood donations from gay men and trans women, but noted that more work is needed. The Minister of Finance discussed replacing the Canada Emergency Response Benefit (CERB) with the wage subsidy, incentivizing work during economic recovery. A member asked if CERB would be extended with an employment incentive to support tourism workers whose industries will not recover quickly.

The government adapted the Canada emergency response benefit (CERB) by ensuring people who receive royalties aren't penalized under it. A $500 million fund to specifically help the arts and culture sector was announced. The government will be there for Canadian workers in July.

A sole proprietorship funding issue was noted, with Minister Joly stating that Minister Ng's colleague has been working on addressing the issue through regional development agencies for businesses without access to funding through banks. The government remains committed to keeping communities safe and continues to support farmers with hundreds of millions of dollars in support. The House has been recalled.

education_13.transcript: Lynne Neagle AM: Good afternoon, everyone. Welcome to the Children, Young People and Education Committee. We've received apologies for absence from Hefin David and Jack Sargeant. Vikki Howells is substituting for Jack Sargeant. So, Vikki, welcome; it's good to see you in the committee. Item 2 this afternoon is our eleventh evidence session on the Children (Abolition of Defence of Reasonable Punishment) (Wales) Bill. I'm very pleased to welcome Barry Hughes, who is Chief Crown Prosecutor for Wales; Kwame Biney, who is senior policy advisor, CPS; and Iwan Jenkins, who is head of the complex casework unit, Crown Prosecution Service Cymru Wales. So thank you all for attending this afternoon. We're really looking forward to hearing your views on the Bill. If you're happy, we'll go straight into questions from Members, and the first ones are from Siân Gwenllian. Barry Hughes: Perfectly happy. Sian Gwenllian AM: Thank you very much. I would like to start just by looking in general at how the law currently stands, and how do you think the law as it currently stands today, and specifically in terms of reasonable punishment—how does that protect children. Barry Hughes: Sorry, can I just be clear? How does the law as it presently stands protect children? Sian Gwenllian AM: Yes. Barry Hughes: We have a range of offences created by the criminal law, going back to the Offences Against the Person Act 1861 in the middle of the century before last, which provide for offences of assault against a variety of people, including, in particular, Acts such as the Children and Young Persons Act 1933, which provides for offences that are specific to children. But the more general criminal legislation, such as the Offences Against the Person Act, does provide specifically for adults and children alike not to be subject to physical assault. In respect of children in particular, you'll be aware that we have section 58 of the Children Act 2004, which does make it a defence for a person accused of assaulting a child to argue that it was a case where they were acting only so as to impose reasonable punishment upon the child. That is a defence that is available for assault upon a child. By way of contrast, it is not available for an offence involving an adult. In that regard, it could be argued that children have fewer rights than adults. Sian Gwenllian AM: Well, a review was undertaken by the Government back in 2007 that stated that retaining the law in its current form—that that would be the best thing to do because there's no evidence that it's not working satisfactorily. You are suggesting perhaps towards the end of your answer that there could be some change that's happened since 2007. Barry Hughes: I think the law should reflect contemporary society and the law is evolutionary in nature, and should be evolutionary in nature. And just as if we go back 50 years there are types of behaviour that we might all have contemplated back then as being socially acceptable, many of those types of behaviour are not now, and the law has changed to reflect that. We don't need to go back a very long way to find, for example, that there was no such offence as raping one's married wife, and the law has changed to reflect the fact that that is simply not acceptable. And from what I've seen of the research, and I think back to the 2007 review, to which you've just referred, there is evidence within that review already that opinions are changing. And I've seen research more current than that that suggests that young people find it less acceptable to use reasonable punishment in chastising a child than do people of an older generation. Of course, as we move forward in time, the people who are young now are going to be the older adults of the future. So one would hope that the law will change to reflect the way in which people behave within society. Sian Gwenllian AM: Okay, thank you. We have heard evidence as a committee that this proposed law would provide a much clearer legal framework—that is, to take out this reasonable punishment defence, that then would make things much clearer for parents and professionals. Does the CPS have a view on that? Barry Hughes: I think it will make things clearer. I'm not sure it will make them much clearer, because if we get into some of the—. It may be helpful to look at some examples at some stage. And when it comes to prosecuting, it's often the case that things aren't just black and white; there are multiple shades of grey in between. We know that the Children Act did introduce a degree of clarity, in that it removed the defence of reasonable chastisement for offences of causing actual bodily harm, causing grievous bodily harm, causing grievous bodily harm with intent, and various incidents of child cruelty under the Children and Young Persons Act. In that sense, matters were clarified, because up until that point it was not entirely clear. There were a series of Court of Appeal cases that sought to clarify the law, but that was at a time, at the end of the 1990s, the start of the 2000s, when we had the European convention on human rights and we had a quickly developing body of case law. And the Children Act was brought in to reflect the direction that the case law was moving in. A decision was clearly made back then in the early 2000s not to include common assault in the same category as actual bodily harm, grievous bodily harm et cetera. So, there was a decision made back then that it was appropriate to do that. As we wind forward some 15 years, I have seen the research that suggests that some people see that the present legislation, and the way in which section 58 applies to reasonable chastisement for common assault of children—some people have interpreted that as saying that smacking is acceptable. I don't think that it is what it says, but there is a degree of confusion around that. And the reason why I say it's not as simple as that is, going back to my words a couple of minutes ago, it's rarely a case of being black and white; there are these degrees of grey. And removing that defence does make life clearer. Sian Gwenllian AM: What are the areas of grey that you refer to? Can you give a few examples? Barry Hughes: If you take an example—so, one of the examples that I've heard discussed has been a child—a young child; let's say a five-year-old child by the side of a road—who darts to go out in the path of moving traffic. The parent grabs hold of the child and pulls them back. Now, technically, taking hold of the child at that point is an assault, because it's an unwanted imposition of physical force. But no-one's going to say that's anything like an assault that should come to the criminal courts. Of course, that would be utter nonsense. There's no hostility; it's simply taking hold of a child to protect them. At that point, the parent then seeks to admonish the child, tells them off and smacks the child on the legs. Now, that would probably be seen under the present law as reasonable chastisement. Probably. But so much depends upon the circumstances, whether this is something that happens frequently with the child, the size of the child, the parent, the force of the slap, the way in which it's imposed. So, that, I would say, sits at one end of the spectrum. As we move towards the other end of the spectrum, we might have repeated smacking, shouting, maybe hitting the child over the head as well. So, there is a spectrum of offending. Towards one end of the spectrum we move much closer towards something that would be criminal in nature. There'll be a point at which it crosses the threshold from something that needs to be reported to the police, and the police would take some form of action, and then, as we move through the tiers, it'll reach a stage where the police think this is a case that should be prosecuted and they would bring it to the Crown Prosecution Service for a charging decision. We would then decide the most appropriate means of dealing with it. And the absolute other end of the spectrum here is one where we decide it's an offence that we think is so serious that it should be charged and brought before a criminal court. That would be very much towards the serious end of the spectrum of the type of behaviour that I've just described to you. Does that help to illustrate that, at one end, it's relatively white and the other end it's relatively black, but in the middle we have the shades of grey? Sian Gwenllian AM: But that would mean that there could be more prosecutions at the softer end. Because if you're removing this—we'll probably come on to that. Barry Hughes: Shall I address that in terms—? Okay. So, you are probably aware, but forgive me if I just explain quickly anyway, when we approach a file of material evidence submitted by the police we apply the code for Crown prosecutors, which has a two-stage test. The first stage is whether there's sufficient evidence to provide a realistic prospect of conviction, and the second stage is—. And you only get to the second stage if the first stage is satisfied. If there isn't enough evidence, we don't go on to consider whether it's in the public interest, because we wouldn't put an offence before the courts if we didn't think there was a realistic prospect of conviction. So, we only get on to the public interest stage once the evidential stage is satisfied. So, to return to the point, if we have an offence where, let's say, there is a light smack at the time, the police apply the same code before they bring a case to us. We don't always agree with the police; generally we do, but we don't always agree. It's a matter for them whether they refer a matter to the Crown Prosecution Service. So, if a police officer takes witness statements in relation to that case—the light smacking on the leg—at present it's unlikely that would come to the CPS, because they would look at it and say, 'Reasonable chastisement provides for a defence.' If that defence is removed, then obviously there is a greater possibility that it would be referred to the CPS. I would like to think—and I think this is what will probably happen in practice—that the police would take a view that the evidential test may have been satisfied because the defence had been removed, but it wouldn't be in the public interest to prosecute. It may be that the police decide that it is—it may have been two smacks, three smacks, so it moves towards the end of the spectrum that would suggest that matters are becoming rather more serious. So, it may be referred to the CPS for a charging decision. We would then apply independently the same test, and we would probably conclude that the evidential stage was met in that instance because the defence no longer exists, which takes us on to considering the public interest. In the circumstances that I've described, every case is going to be unique on its own facts, but in the circumstances that I've described, if it is just a light smack and it's a one-off and there's no history of this, it would probably be the sort of offence we'd decide it wouldn't be in the public interest to prosecute. Sian Gwenllian AM: But isn't it creating a new grey area in terms of the police now having to decide whether repeated smacking is in the public interest to at least refer it to you to decide about that? Do you know what I'm saying? Barry Hughes: If this Bill goes through, then it will remove a defence, which will make it likely that the police will give this more consideration for referral to the CPS than beforehand, but it doesn't necessarily mean they will refer it, because it will depend on the facts of each individual case. Sian Gwenllian AM: Thanks. Lynne Neagle AM: We've got some questions now on the divergence between the law in England and Wales from Dawn Bowden. Dawn Bowden AM: Thank you, Chair. Hello. The Assembly doesn't have any legislative competence to impose duties on the CPS, as you know, in the absence of the Secretary of State's consent. What are the implications for implementing the Bill in Wales for that? Barry Hughes: Can I give you two examples? One of the implications is this—and it's fairly obvious but it's still worth stressing—that the removal of the defence would mean that there would be more cases that would pass the evidential stage in Wales than there would be in England, because the defence would still apply there. That, therefore, raises issues in particular in terms of awareness of criminal offending for people from England who travel to Wales, because it's fairly evident that there would be a defence available in England that is not available in Wales. One of the examples that we talked about was if you consider a family on its way from England into Wales, travelling to Barry Island for a holiday, and troublesome children in the back of the car, harsh words and a small smack on the leg delivered while they're on the M4 going past Bristol would be subject to a defence. By the time we come over to this side of the water, there would be no such defence for the same journey, for the same act. And so, there are issues there in terms of the awareness of members of the public. The second example that I'll give you is that we already have within the United Kingdom a certain degree of divergence of laws, and the example I'll give to you is this: drink driving. In England and Wales, the limit for having micrograms of alcohol in your breath is 35 µg in 100 ml of breath. Across the border in Scotland, it drops to 22 µg. So, the same act—. As you drive into Scotland, you're probably okay to drive if you've got 34 µg; by the time you get into Scotland, you won't be. And the consequences for you are significant, because of course it's an offence in Scotland, and not an offence in England, but the punishment, namely disqualification, applies everywhere in the United Kingdom. So, we already have a degree of divergence of law, and the CPS recognises this. In our code, we have a specific provision that takes account of the potential divergence of Welsh law. We will issue policy guidance and charging standards to reflect any changes as they arise. And we think that we are sufficiently flexible to take account of that. Dawn Bowden AM: Okay. So, would you see the necessity for a memorandum of understanding, for instance, or it being useful in these circumstances, or are you quite satisfied that the current guidance code would cover that? Barry Hughes: Well, it's yet to move from a Bill into an Act, so we'd need to make sure that happened. We know there would be a period of time, perhaps as much as two years, or maybe a little more, while that takes place. There will be a further series of awareness raising. We have strategic implementation groups—Iwan sits on that group. So, we would build ourselves up to come up with the appropriate guidance according to the circumstances. In terms of how we'd approach it within the Crown Prosecution Service in Cymru-Wales, my approach would be along the lines of working with Kwame and his colleagues to make sure that the guidance that we issue nationally suits both England and Wales. And when it comes to the practical application of the legislation in Wales, we would almost certainly work on the basis that the number of offences—and we might want to get into the number of potential offences, but my take on it is the number of offences is likely to be very small and we would probably have two or three specialists trained in this so that any case that comes through goes to people who've got a close network and can talk with each other— Dawn Bowden AM: And that would be primarily around the public interest issue. Barry Hughes: It would. Dawn Bowden AM: I understand. Okay. So you're obviously a key stakeholder in terms of this legislation and you're clearly very au fait with it and all its implications. What's actually been your involvement with the Government as they've been developing this legislation? Barry Hughes: My own personal involvement? Dawn Bowden AM: Or the service's. Barry Hughes: Iwan and colleagues have had some involvement on a more routine basis. I met with a Minister and a Deputy Minister—I met with Huw Irranca-Davies a little while ago, and I met with Julie Morgan a few months ago—to discuss the overall implications, and then officials on both sides have been engaging with each other. It hasn't been a very close-knit involvement, but we have had enough involvement so that we've felt we've been able to offer views on how we would deal with the legislation. Dawn Bowden AM: Okay. So you've been satisfied with the level of involvement that you've had. Barry Hughes: Yes. I don't feel that it's been too much, nor do I feel we've been left on the side at all. Dawn Bowden AM: Okay. Now, we've just talked about the divergence of criminal law, and this is probably the first time that there's been an obvious divergence between English and Welsh law, and so we were then talking about the guidance, weren't we, and whether that needs amending to reflect it. But I think you were saying that you felt, within the existing guidance, it could be dealt with, so you don't see a need to update or amend the guidance. Barry Hughes: I think we would need to update the guidance, and it goes back to the application of the evidential stage, before the public interest stage. On the evidential stage, I doubt that we'll need to do much, other than to make people aware that the defence is not available in Wales in the same way that it is in England, and we might have some light-touch guidance around that. When it comes to the public interest stage, that's a little more specialist and I think we would need to develop more with respect to that. Dawn Bowden AM: And are you satisfied that your staff in Wales—well, and in England, actually, across the border—will be sufficiently aware of the divergence in this area of law? Barry Hughes: Not as of the present day. Give us a chance. [Laughter.] Dawn Bowden AM: But you would expect that to be something you would— Barry Hughes: Yes, I have no doubt. To an extent, actually, I think I would say that staff in Wales are aware of this already, because in my role as chief Crown prosecutor, I publish a monthly blog and we talk about the things we're doing, so staff are aware. I've discussed with staff and they've seen my blog and we've put it on our website: there have been engagements with the Welsh Government that this is a piece of work in which we're interested and that is under way. So, actually, I'm probably being unfair to myself. I think most staff would probably be aware that we are involved in this and that it is something that, at present, may well happen. Dawn Bowden AM: Okay. And does that mean that there would also be a similar need for the CPS in England to be aware of the changes? Barry Hughes: They would need to be aware, but not to the same extent. We have regular interchange of prosecutors between England and Wales. So, in my office in Cardiff, for example, because we work digitally, I have about 30 to 35 prosecutors who work for the CPS in London, and they work exclusively on London cases. But there are times when some of those prosecutors will prosecute at courts in Wales, and then there may be an interchange; they'll come to work for us on secondment, or they'll come to work for us permanently. We'd need to make sure that those people were brought up to speed on the fact that there are some aspects of Welsh law that diverge from English law. Dawn Bowden AM: That's helpful, thank you. My final question, Chair, is just whether you think there are any other significant cross-border issues that arise from this potential legislation. Barry Hughes: In addition to those that I've mentioned, probably not. It boils down to the fact that there's a defence in England for assaults upon children that isn't available in Wales if this Bill goes through, and then it's dealing with the consequences of that. Dawn Bowden AM: Okay. That's fine. Thank you, Chair. Lynne Neagle AM: I've got a supplementary from Suzy, and then Siân. Suzy Davies AM: Thank you. I was going to ask this question later, but I think it fits in—. I've forgotten what I was going to ask; this is terrible. Oh no, that's right—obviously, this Act is on a particular trajectory at the moment, and it's due to be introduced before the end of this Assembly. Do you think it would be better for us as scrutinisers of this Act if we could see the draft changes to CPS guidance on the public interest test before we make our final decision? Barry Hughes: I honestly don't think that would necessarily be helpful. I've had some discussions with Kwame, who would have an involvement in this. What we would envisage is that we would simply want to take the present public interest factors, which are set out, in my view, very clearly in the code for Crown prosecutors, and we would provide a degree of detail around those that relates more specifically to the issues that we're discussing here. So, it would be taking principles of generality and according them a degree of specificity. And we'd need to work that up as we go along, and I think you'd run a risk of putting the cart before the horse, if I may put it like that. Suzy Davies AM: It's just that, personally, I think the public interest test is critical in all this, and it would really help us to understand what it could look like before we commit to a particular course of action in supporting or not supporting the Bill, that was all. But thank you, anyway; I take your point. Lynne Neagle AM: Siân. Sian Gwenllian AM: My point is about the criminal law in Wales and England diverging in this particular instance, and we've been told that this is probably the first time it's happened. Can you think of any other examples where that divergence has happened and does it actually set some kind of precedent in motion? Barry Hughes: There are other offences in Wales that don't exist in England, but they don't crop up very often. So, to give you examples, there is an offence of putting an electronic collar on your pet—let's not stretch that into children. [Laughter.] But, you know, sometimes—I'll be quiet. But putting an electronic collar on your pet that administers a shock is an offence in Wales; it's not an offence in England. There are also offences around the picking of cockles in west Wales, which is not an offence—. So, you can see there are some, but I would argue that the fact that you drive across the border into Scotland and commit an offence that you won't commit in England is possibly more significant for the general public. Of course, the topic we're discussing here is of real relevance for the general public, and you have heard evidence, I know, already, that comes from people who are quite strongly in favour and people quite strongly against it. One of the ways in which I've approached this is looking at it from the perspective of what the law is there for, which is to set out, in essence, what is acceptable, what is not acceptable. It doesn't necessarily mean we need to use the law for that, but it signifies how society views a certain behaviour. I like to look at it from the perspective of the person who is being affected by this, namely the child, and the change in the law here would afford children a degree of protection that they don't presently have, but which they do have when they attain the age of 16.FootnoteLink So, I looked at this—. If I can give you another example. I've had considerable dealings over the years with offences against residents in care homes, and you'll have seen Winterbourne View, you'll have seen recently up in Durham, where we have adults who need to be looked after because of issues with their mental capacity, and sometimes, the carers become frustrated with them and they admonish them. Sometimes they admonish them verbally and then that turns into physical admonishment. In much the same way as children are vulnerable and are looked after and can be sometimes quite annoying—but there is a level of protection for an adult striking a child lightly under the present law that is not available to the same adult for striking that child when they turn 16. That seems to me to not necessarily fit with where we ought to be as a society. Sian Gwenllian AM: Do you think that what we're doing in Wales will be emulated in England at some point? Barry Hughes: Well, it's certainly going through in parallel in Scotland, and there are a great many other countries around the world that already have this, including a large number of European countries. Who knows? Trying to get any legislation through Westminster at the moment—who knows? Not tomorrow, I'd say. Lynne Neagle AM: We've got some specific questions around implementation now from Suzy Davies. Suzy Davies AM: Thank you. Can I just begin by asking you to help the committee out here in understanding how the burden of proof is going to work if this defence is removed? Because the issue of whether something is punishment or not is going to disappear, providing that it's clear that contact with a child has happened. Is that going to work on the presumption that that's an unlawful contact? Is that something the prosecution will still have to prove, or will it be for an accused parent to say, to use your example, 'I was just dragging them out of the traffic or stopping them putting their hands in the fire'? Barry Hughes: I understand your point. There's no change whatsoever to the burden of proof, nor to the standard of proof. Suzy Davies AM: Perhaps you can run us through it quickly. Barry Hughes: If the defence argue that that act was—. So, we have to show that there's been an unlawful assault. So, if we remove the defence of reasonable chastisement, in a sense that alters some things but it doesn't alter the basic responsibility of the prosecution, which is to establish its case beyond a reasonable doubt. And if the defence raise an argument and say, 'Well, look, that was a lawful act; I was only doing what I thought was reasonable in the circumstances', it's for the Crown to disprove that. Suzy Davies AM: Okay. That's great. Barry Hughes: And we've got to disprove that to the criminal standard, which is beyond a reasonable doubt. So, you can certainly see—. I can see the potential for individuals who feel strongly about this to look to contest the matter, to not admit any wrongdoing at all and to take the matter to trial, and it would be our responsibility to disprove that. Suzy Davies AM: Okay. That's really helpful for us to understand that. Obviously, when we're talking about CPS guidelines and all the rest, we've already come some distance down the process, haven't we? Have you got any views on what might be done to prevent cases even coming as far as arrest? Because one of the things that we have to consider is that once you're arrested, that is recorded somewhere and will appear in things like DBS checks in the future, even if it goes no further. Do you have any views on how intervention might work better earlier on, even at the point of the knock at the door? Barry Hughes: From the perspective of the CPS, I'm not sure I can help you there. Suzy Davies AM: That's fine. I was expecting that answer. Barry Hughes: Simply because that's a matter for the police. Unlike in many other jurisdictions, we have no power whatsoever to direct the police, so if the police decide not to investigate—. I routinely get letters from members of the public outraged that the police have decided not to investigate their particular neighbour dispute or something, and they want us to do something about it and we can't. We can't direct the police. Suzy Davies AM: Thank you for that, because I thought that was important to get on the record as well. We're looking at a period of a couple of years after this Act passes—if it does—for implementation. Is there anything at the moment that you think perhaps you still need to do as an organisation towards being in a position to practically implement this law? Barry Hughes: I think we need to maintain the type of dialogue that we've built up with the Welsh Government and colleagues there. I think we need to keep abreast of things as they move forward. We need involvement—Iwan, for example, and the strategic implementation group. We have other members of my staff who are involved in the other work streams that feed through to that, and I believe that that involvement will ensure that we are sufficiently interconnected; that we can liaise, in turn, with Kwame and his colleagues in the headquarters to respond to things as they develop. So, I think we've got a network of contacts built up here that is adequate for the purpose—at least, 'adequate' might sound a little— Suzy Davies AM: Sufficient. Barry Hughes: Yes, perfectly sufficient. Suzy Davies AM: So, you're confident, by 2022, then, that you'll have changes to the CPS guidelines that are good to go, if you like. Barry Hughes: Fit for purpose, yes. Suzy Davies AM: Yes. Okay, that's great. Bear with me a second—thank you very much—in your written evidence, you did welcome the indication from the Welsh Government that this reasonable period after Royal Assent would be necessary. Apart from the CPS guideline changes, which you'll be working through, is there anything else you think you might be needing to do apart from just keeping in touch? I'm just thinking: are there any practical and possibly financial implications for your workforce in this? Barry Hughes: If this legislation goes through, there will be minor financial consequences for us and I don't see any significant financial consequences in the period between now and the Bill becoming law other than the time of ourselves in discussing this today. But there isn't anything significant. There's nothing that will impact upon our performance as the public prosecution service in that time. Suzy Davies AM: Okay. And what about afterwards, then? Because one of the pieces of thinking behind this Act is to collect data to see whether this change in the law actually works. Do you want to talk us through a little bit about how that might affect you? I don't know who to ask on this. Barry Hughes: Certainly. Well, I think that we are going to need to maintain a clear record of any cases that are referred to the CPS from the police and that will—. Normally, we do this through our case management system, which is England-and-Wales wide, and you'd put a flag on. So, for example, domestic abuse, there's a flag for that, and assault on a child, there's a flag for that. But this would be a very specific flag for assault on a child—reasonable chastisement. Trying to get that through on a system that covers all of England and Wales—it's quite expensive, unbelievably; it wouldn't happen. So, we would keep a manual log of every referral. That would be unmanageable if we were dealing with hundreds and hundreds of cases. Suzy Davies AM: So, you're not anticipating hundreds and hundreds. Barry Hughes: Not at all. Not at all. My take on it: I've seen and spoken with Matt Jukes about this, from South Wales Police; I have seen the justice impact assessment; I've seen the research that's been fed through from New Zealand; and I've seen our own response to a freedom of information request and the papers that the CPS put into the review that you mentioned earlier in 2007. I would be very surprised if we were to prosecute anything other than low single figures a year, if that much. If that much. We may have more referrals from the police, but I doubt it would be double the figure we prosecute, even. So, you're talking small numbers. Now, small numbers—and we might not have any. Okay? It depends. Obviously, if there's a really good awareness-raising campaign, you make it less likely. And, for us, prosecution—it's not the first port of call here at all. There are out-of-court disposals and there are all sorts of diversions that you will have discussed, and I know you've discussed, with the police and the police and crime commissioner. So, the numbers will be very small, which means we would be able to keep a clear track on those and, obviously, we would wish to keep the situation under review, and we'd reach a period of time after whatever—12, 18 months, maybe 24 months—where we'd look back to see how we are doing. And I'd like to think that we will be doing that in consultation with interested parties and stakeholders. We do this already with certain types of offences. We have what we call local scrutiny involvement panels. So, for offences such as racially aggravated offending, whether it's criminal damage or offence against the person, we will bring together interested parties, by which I mean people from outside the CPS, quite often third sector agencies that represent vulnerable groups. We'd bring them in and look through the cases. So, we open our books, show them our cases, and we have a very frank discussion. We involve the police in that as well. That, in turn, helps us shape and improve the way that we prosecute these cases and I can foresee that, after this legislation came in, we might get 18 months, two years down the road, and I can see us sitting in a room with some of the cases we've prosecuted, maybe cases we've decided not to prosecute, with the police and interested parties from both sides, to have a discussion about how we're doing with it all. And I'd like to think that the Welsh Government would be involved in that as well. Suzy Davies AM: That's really helpful to know because, of course, one of the difficulties of dealing with the culture change, which is what the Minister's effectively after here, is that we are still talking about it in the context of criminal legislation. It's nobody's fault; that's just how it is. And what I'm hearing from you is that the chances of somebody who's currently protected by the defence and is not part of a bigger case where there's serial smacking or other difficulties in a family, for example—the chances of them getting to you in the first place are pretty low, and then the chances of them passing even the current public interest tests are pretty low. In which case, my question is: why are we bothering with this law rather than concentrating on a new piece of law, possibly through the civil system, which would achieve the culture change better? Maybe that's not a question you feel that you can answer, but you can see why I'm asking the question. Barry Hughes: I can attempt an answer. I follow entirely where you're going. I agree, the chances are pretty low, but they're greater than they are presently. In other words, there is a greater degree of protection for children, and I think the value that would derive from this legislation is the message that it sends out. I go back to what I was saying to you right at the start, about the evolution of the law, and the way we've seen many other countries around the world adopt a very similar approach to that being proposed by the Welsh Government is, in my view, an evolution of the law. So, yes, there may not be many more prosecutions, but there won't be fewer than there are presently, because there's a greater degree of protection for children. Suzy Davies AM: Okay, that's really helpful, thank you. Lynne Neagle AM: Before we move on, can I just ask—? The Children Act 2004 applies to a child up to the age of 18— Barry Hughes: Sixteen. Lynne Neagle AM: You mentioned 16; that's what I wanted to clarify. Our understanding was that it went up to 18. Barry Hughes: I'm reasonably confident that it's 16.FootnoteLink Lynne Neagle AM: Okay. Okay, thank you. We've got some questions now specifically on prosecutions and potential alternatives from Vikki Howells. Vikki Howells AM: Thank you, Chair. You've already touched quite a bit on the issue of the public interest test. Is there anything else you'd like to put on record about the factors that the CPS would be taking into consideration when applying the public interest test in respect of this Bill? Barry Hughes: Can I give you an example, which might illustrate the way in which things could be different? This is one, which—I give credit to Iwan for this, because he thought of it. If we take an adult with a small child—say, a four-year-old—in a supermarket, and the child is pulling cans off the shelves, it's quite annoying, and they keep doing it and keep doing it. The parent remonstrates, and the parent ends up finding that verbal admonishment is inadequate, and smacks the child lightly on the legs, which does the trick and the child stops. Presently, if someone in that supermarket were to report that to the police, I think the police would probably conclude that that is covered by the defence of reasonable chastisement. In other words, the evidential stage wouldn't be passed; you don't get on to consider the public interest. But if we have that same parent in there the following week, and you have the same happen again, presently, the defence would apply. Now, there comes a point in time when the defence stops applying. If that defence didn't apply at all, and we had a sequence of three offences, the public interest test would kick in, if I may put it like that, pretty quickly, because it would be repeated behaviour. So, that would be the distinction between how things are now and how they would be in the future, because the defence would not be available, so the public interest test would then come to the fore that much more quickly. So, when we apply the public interest test, the essence of it is about proportionality, and trying to come up with an approach that is proportionate to the offending. We look at the age of the offender, the age of the victim, we look at the circumstances, we look at the impact upon the victim, we look at the impact that's likely in relation to the suspect, and there is, within the code, a long list of matters that we take into account. Some of them are fairly obvious, such as previous convictions. If someone's got previous convictions for doing something wrong, it's more likely they're going to be prosecuted the next time. But there's no part of the law that says that prosecution will always follow. Mostly, it's reasonably obvious; the more serious an offence, the more likely you are to be prosecuted.
