New teachers lack experience to deal with challenging behaviour, leading to a high turnover rate, but some schools have implemented successful mentorship programs and policies to improve consistency.

Concerns were raised about the increasing trend of younger children being excluded from school, with possible links to adverse childhood experiences and behavioural issues. The role of accountability measures in driving exclusions was questioned, with a need for further qualitative research on the topic.

The discussion focused on providing adequate support for pupils at risk of becoming EOTAS (Elective Home Education) students, with a whole-school approach being seen as crucial. Statutory provision for this approach is proposed.

The committee discussed the importance of addressing adverse childhood experiences in education, recommending training for teachers and a statutory curriculum on health and well-being. Schools should be inclusive and provide proper support, balancing funding between diagnosis and provision.

The committee discussed the importance of addressing adverse childhood experiences in the curriculum, particularly for vulnerable groups such as young men with early trauma, and emphasized the need for a preventative approach to mental health support.

The panel discussed concerns about self-harm and mental health issues among young people, particularly girls aged 15-19, and noted varying support levels in schools depending on senior management team commitment. Off-rolling pupils with mental health problems is a concern, especially in PRU provision.

Liz Williams discussed how "naughty" children are often those with undiagnosed communication difficulties and anxiety issues. Formal diagnosis can help provide necessary support, especially through a whole-school approach to emotional well-being.

The discussion centered on the impact of exclusion from education on young people, with a focus on exacerbating pre-existing inequalities. There is concern about home schooling's lack of transparency and its potential to hide vulnerable children who may be at risk of adverse effects.

There is a lack of understanding about exclusions and their impact on mental wellbeing, particularly among vulnerable young people. Support for excluded children is inconsistent and often fragmented across different institutions, with unclear responsibilities among schools, local authorities, and parents.
The importance of a whole-school approach to mental health was highlighted, with a focus on foregrounding it within school ethos and intervening early to prevent issues from arising.
education_4.summary: The meeting focused on addressing the rise in exclusions and exploring ways to support vulnerable learners. Challenging behaviour and additional learning needs were identified as contributing factors, with local authorities developing strategies for support and reintegration. However, capacity issues and funding challenges hindered consistent implementation across all areas. The importance of a whole-family approach was emphasized, along with the need for training on adverse childhood experiences and trauma-informed practice. Retaining pupils in mainstream schools was also discussed, with concerns raised about variation in provision between terms and weeks, as well as high delegation levels and staff pressures. A model for retaining pupils in school settings was proposed, involving partnerships within schools to keep money in schools and prevent learners being moved around. The meeting highlighted the need for teacher training, including experience working in PRUs, and addressed concerns about quality assurance of alternative provision, inconsistent provision outside of PRUs, and off-rolling of students.
refined_chunk_summaries: The panel suggests that challenging behaviour and additional learning needs may be contributing factors to the rise in exclusions. Local authorities are developing strategies for support and reintegration, but there is a lag in implementing them consistently across all areas due to insufficient capacity. School accountability measures place pressures on schools.

The discussion highlighted the importance of a whole-family approach in supporting families with complex needs. Training on adverse childhood experiences and trauma-informed practice has been provided to schools. Funding challenges are placing pressure on schools, particularly due to high delegation levels and staff pressures. The introduction of the new Act may exacerbate these pressures.

Variation in provision between terms and weeks creates challenges for retaining pupils in mainstream schools, potentially leading to exclusions. The new curriculum coming in provides an opportunity to agree on a broader range of measures. Getting children back into mainstream education at key stage 4 is more challenging due to the type of learners involved.

A model for retaining pupils in school settings has been tried elsewhere, including Oxfordshire, where a local authority looked at partnerships within schools to keep money in schools and prevent learners being moved around. Capacity issues are caused by not having enough spaces in some cases. Nick Williams said safeguarding is at the forefront of their thinking, but providing additional training to staff in EOTAS provision is a challenge.

Quality assurance of alternative provision can be challenging due to difficulties in accessing home settings. Providers may use their own staff for alternative provision, which is seen as a way to address concerns about quality assurance. The WLGA paper expresses concerns about changes to pupil registration and their implications for the management of data/funding. There are efforts to move away from off-rolling of students, focusing on supporting vulnerable learners.

The committee expresses concern about inconsistency in provision of education for pupils with additional needs outside of PRUs and local authority commissioned tuition. David Hopkins acknowledges that it is a concern when home tuition is provided by third-party commissioning arrangements. Within the local authority maintained sector, if provision is not being made to meet all pupils' needs, the issue may arise.

Nick Williams emphasized the need for teacher training to include experience working in PRUs. Sharon Davies highlighted the limited opportunities for networking and training among providers. Nick Williams mentioned that local authorities have met with health boards to discuss partnerships. A general deficit in child and adolescent mental health services may be contributing to more pupils having additional needs, which could translate into those ending up in EOTAS provisions.

New teachers lack experience dealing with challenging behaviour. Nick Williams notes that it's not just this reason for people leaving the profession. Schools use a mentor to ensure consistency across the whole school, and policies are in place to promote good practice. Janet Finch-Saunders raises concerns about transport issues for EOTAS learners in rural areas, citing large distances as a problem.

Concerns were raised about an increase in younger children being excluded, which has gone up substantially in the past few years. Dr Ian Johnson noted that underlying factors influencing exclusions may include behavioural issues and adverse childhood experiences. The role of accountability measures in driving exclusions was highlighted as an area for further exploration.

The discussion highlighted the importance of a whole-school approach in supporting pupils at risk of becoming EOTAS, which should be statutory provision. This approach aims to prevent exclusion and address adverse childhood experiences by understanding the child's individual needs within the school environment, with consequences for exclusion including an elevated risk of suicide if not addressed.

The committee discussed addressing adverse childhood experiences in education, including teacher training and a proposed new curriculum on health, well-being, mental health, and awareness. Sarah Stone emphasized the importance of young people's voices and skills in understanding emotions. Liz Williams highlighted concerns about participants' lack of awareness and knowledge of children with additional learning needs, noting that up to 50% may have these needs.

The committee discussed the importance of addressing adverse childhood experiences in schools and education in Wales, particularly for vulnerable groups who have experienced early trauma. Dr Ian Johnson mentioned his previous experience as a school governor where he saw the establishment of a trauma-informed approach being widened. The panel emphasized intervening early to prevent lifelong isolation and address the impact of early trauma on individuals' mental health and well-being.

The panel discussed concerns about self-harm and mental health issues among young people, particularly how girls experience this within the school environment and in general, noting quite substantial increases in some cases. Support levels in schools depend on the senior management team's commitment. Off-rolling pupils with mental health problems is a concern, potentially more prevalent in EOTAS or PRU provision.

Liz Williams discussed how children with communication difficulties and anxiety issues are often labelled "naughty" due to a lack of understanding from teachers. The label itself becomes an impediment to getting support. Dr Ian Johnson highlighted concerns about learners feeling uncomfortable disclosing problems due to fear of not being taken seriously. The Estyn report notes that there are generally fewer off-rolling from Welsh-medium education to EOTAS, potentially due to socio-economic profiles and lack of EOTAS provision within a local area.

The discussion centered on delays for young people not in education or training and how these delays exacerbate inequalities and disadvantage. Concerns were raised about home schooling's lack of transparency, which makes vulnerable children "hidden or invisible". The panel also discussed individual tuition, including the need to support students reintroduced into mainstream education if needed.

Support for excluded children is often fragmented across different institutions. The responsibility lies with local education authorities in this context. Parents must know where to go for support themselves and need clear information about their child's rights and entitlements. A national picture of support for these children is lacking.

Dr Ian Johnson emphasized the importance of a statutory whole-school approach in foregrounding mental health and emotional well-being within school ethos. He highlighted that considering mental health and emotional well-being first is key to improving the well-being of future generations. Sarah Stone discussed the link between inequality, adverse childhood experiences, and the opportunity to intervene positively to prevent young people from falling out of school and being socially abandoned.

chunk_feedback:
Here are the flagged sentences:

ISSUE 1:
The panel agrees that challenging behaviour and unmet additional learning needs contribute to the rise in exclusions, with the system struggling to keep up with demand.

REASON: The transcript does not mention "unmet additional learning needs" as a contributing factor. Nick Williams mentions "additional learning needs" but also notes that the system is struggling to provide enough capacity for it.

ISSUE 2:
Local authorities have developed strategies for support and reintegration, but there is a lag in implementing consistent provision across all authorities.

REASON: The transcript does not mention a "lag in implementing consistent provision across all authorities". Nick Williams mentions a "little bit of lag in the system" referring to bringing everybody up to the same level, but it's unclear if this is a lag in implementation or a lag in achieving consistency.

ISSUE 3:
School accountability measures are seen as contributing to pressures on schools, which may lead to exclusions.

REASON: The transcript does not clearly imply that school accountability measures directly contribute to exclusions. Sharon Davies mentions that "there's a huge amount of pressures on schools to perform" and Nick Williams mentions that the government is conveying messages about the importance of progression over just meeting targets.

After reviewing the summary against the transcript section, I've flagged some sentences that contain factual errors. Here are the results:

ISSUE [1]: The discussion focused on the impact of funding challenges and the new legislation on exclusions, particularly for students with additional learning needs.

REASON: The transcript actually mentions "exclusions" but also includes discussions about adverse childhood experiences and trauma-informed practice, which were not mentioned as a focus in this sentence.

ISSUE [2]: Local authorities acknowledged pressure on schools to make hard choices due to budget constraints, potentially leading to increased exclusions.

REASON: While Sharon Davies did mention that "sometimes, they have to look at every aspect within the school" and that the new Act is placing more pressure on the system, there is no explicit acknowledgment of "pressure on schools to make hard choices" from local authorities.

Here are the flagged issues:

ISSUE [1]:
David Hopkins said that it's difficult to know how headteachers and governing bodies will react. However, the summary states "It shouldn't, but it could" as a fact about their reaction.
REASON: The transcript does not state that they "shouldn't" react in a particular way; instead, it presents this as an assumption.

ISSUE [2]:
The summary states that collaborative working between schools, PRUs, and local authorities can help provide a continuum of support. However, the transcript mentions that Nick Williams says this is already happening to some extent.
REASON: The transcript section implies that some progress has been made in collaborative working, but it does not state that there are no issues or difficulties.

ISSUE [3]:
The summary states that the new curriculum provides opportunities for improved practices. However, Sharon Davies mentions that getting children back into mainstream education at key stage 4 is more challenging.
REASON: The transcript section presents both points as true and nuanced, without stating that the new curriculum will definitely lead to improved practices.

ISSUE [4]:
The summary states "Collaborative working between schools, PRUs, and local authorities can help provide a continuum of support", which implies that this is not happening yet. However, Nick Williams says that networking within the PRU sector has already improved.
REASON: The transcript section indicates that some progress has been made in collaborative working, but it does not state that there are no issues or difficulties.

ISSUE [5]:
The summary states "Variation in provision between terms and weeks creates challenges for retaining pupils in mainstream schools, potentially leading to exclusions." This is mostly supported by the transcript. However, Nick Williams also mentions that some schools might have spare funding because of planning ahead.
REASON: The transcript section presents both points as true, but it highlights a nuance that some schools might be better prepared than others.

Since I found several issues, I will not respond with "NO_ISSUES".

Here are the results:

ISSUE [1]: The discussion focused on retaining pupils in school settings and exploring alternative models, with Oxfordshire's approach being cited as an example.
REASON: David Hopkins mentioned that at least one authority is looking at how to retain all pupils, but it was unclear if Oxfordshire was specifically involved. Later, Sharon Davies mentioned a local authority in Oxford discussing a partnership within schools.

ISSUE [2]: Local authorities mentioned devolving funding to schools.
REASON: While Nick Williams and David Hopkins did discuss devolving or delegating funding to secondary schools, the term "local authorities" as a whole does not mention this. 

ISSUE [3]: but acknowledged capacity issues and challenges in accessing EOTAS provision due to safeguarding concerns.
REASON: The transcript states that there are capacity issues, but the primary concern for Nick Williams is access to additional provision due to safeguarding reasons and also having providers coming forward.

chunk_summaries_after: The panel agrees that challenging behaviour contributes to the rise in exclusions. Local authorities have developed strategies for support and reintegration, with some experiencing a lag in bringing everybody up to the same level. School accountability measures are placing pressures on schools.

The discussion focused on the impact of funding challenges and the new legislation on exclusions. Local authorities acknowledged that schools are being placed under pressure due to budget constraints, particularly with the introduction of the new Act. This is likely to lead to increased exclusions for students with additional learning needs.

Variation in provision between terms and weeks creates challenges for retaining pupils in mainstream schools, potentially leading to exclusions. Some progress has been made in collaborative working between schools, PRUs, and local authorities through networking and professional learning opportunities. The new curriculum provides opportunities for improved practices, but getting children back into mainstream education at key stage 4 is more challenging.

The discussion explored alternative models for retaining pupils in school settings, with Sharon Davies mentioning a local authority in Oxfordshire discussing partnerships within schools. David Hopkins mentioned that at least one authority is looking at pupil retention, but its involvement was unclear. Capacity issues and challenges accessing EOTAS provision due to safeguarding concerns were also discussed.
Local authorities may play a role in quality assuring alternative provision, particularly individual tuition. However, this can be challenging due to difficulties in accessing home settings.

The committee expresses concern about quality assurance in home tuition provided by third-party commissioning arrangements, particularly for pupils with additional needs, where it becomes increasingly difficult to guarantee that their needs are met. David Hopkins suggests that within the local authority maintained sector, provision should be being made to meet all pupils' needs.

The discussion focused on the challenges faced by EOTAS provisions in Wales, including capacity and staff expertise issues. Nick Williams mentioned that teacher training should prioritize knowledge of working with EOTAS learners, while Sharon Davies noted the importance of networks and conferences for sharing expertise between providers. Local authorities have met with health boards to establish partnerships for supporting EOTAS learners' well-being.

New teachers lack experience dealing with challenging behaviour, but Nick Williams notes that this is not the only reason for people leaving the profession. Some schools have implemented policies to assure consistency across the whole school, including the use of a mentor in good practice.

Concerns were raised about Dr Ian Johnson's concern regarding increases amongst younger children in particular. He noted that this trend has gone up substantially in recent years and suggested a possible link to behavioural issues. The role of accountability measures in driving exclusions was highlighted as an area for further exploration, with Dr Ian Johnson suggesting the need for more qualitative work on the topic.

The discussion focused on providing adequate support for pupils in PRU or EOTAS provision and those at risk of exclusion. A whole-school approach is seen as crucial to addressing this issue. 

(I corrected ISSUE 1 and removed ISSUE 2's sentence as it was phrased as a suggestion rather than a proposal)

The committee discussed addressing adverse childhood experiences in education, including teacher training and a new curriculum on health and well-being. Schools should be inclusive and provide proper support. A participant noted that up to 50 per cent of learners in a classroom may have an additional learning need. The balance between diagnosis and provision was highlighted as an important consideration.

The committee discussed the importance of addressing adverse childhood experiences in the curriculum, particularly for vulnerable groups who have experienced early trauma, and emphasized the need for a preventative approach to mental health support by intervening in existing challenges.

The panel discussed concerns about self-harm and mental health issues among young people, particularly noting an increasing focus on self-harm behaviour amongst girls in the 15 to 19 age range. Support levels in schools depend strongly on senior management team commitment where they are actively involved. Off-rolling pupils with mental health problems is a concern across various settings, including PRU provision.

Liz Williams discussed how "naughty" children are often those with communication difficulties and anxiety issues. Formal diagnosis can help provide necessary support by opening doors for school support. A whole-school approach to emotional well-being is also crucial. Dr Ian Johnson highlighted concerns about learners disclosing problems due to fear of not being believed or accepted. There's a need for investigation into off-rolling, particularly at year 10/11.

The discussion centered on the impact of exclusion from education on young people and its potential to exacerbate pre-existing inequalities. There are concerns about home schooling's lack of transparency and how it may hide vulnerable children who could be at risk. The panel also discussed individual tuition and its potential effects, including the need for support and reintroduction into mainstream education if needed.

There is a lack of understanding about exclusions and their impact on mental well-being, particularly among vulnerable young people. Support for excluded children can be fragmented across different institutions, with unclear responsibilities among local authorities and schools. The responsibility lies with local education authorities in this context. Parents play an important role in supporting their children, but need clear information to do so effectively.

A whole-school approach to mental health was highlighted as key to improving well-being of future generations by Dr Ian Johnson, who emphasized its importance in being statutory and foregrounding it within school ethos. Sarah Stone discussed the link between inequality, adverse childhood experiences, and interventions that can prevent young people from falling out of school and society.

chunk_summaries_after: The panel suggests that challenging behaviour, combined with additional learning needs, contributes to the rise in exclusions. Many local authorities are developing strategies for support and reintegration, but there is a lag in implementing them consistently across all areas. School accountability measures place pressures on schools.

The discussion touched on various topics including a whole-family approach to supporting families with complex needs and training for staff. Funding challenges, particularly due to the introduction of the new Act, are placing pressure on schools. Local authorities have acknowledged that budget constraints may lead to increased exclusions for students with additional learning needs.

Variation in provision between terms and weeks creates challenges for retaining pupils in mainstream schools, potentially leading to exclusions. The new curriculum is seen as an opportunity to improve practices, but getting children back into mainstream education at key stage 4 is more difficult due to being closer to leaving statutory education.

A local authority in Oxfordshire discussed partnerships within schools as part of a model to retain pupils in school settings. David Hopkins mentioned that at least one authority is looking at pupil retention, but he wasn't allowed to say which authority was involved. Capacity issues were also discussed due to insufficient spaces in some year groups. Nick Williams said safeguarding concerns and the need for robust systems are challenges accessing EOTAS provision.

Quality assurance of alternative provision can be challenging due to difficulties in accessing home and private settings. Providers may use their own staff for alternative provision but quality assuring these arrangements is a concern for local authorities. The WLGA is concerned about changes to pupil registration and their implications for data management, funding, and joint working. There are efforts to move away from off-rolling of students who require additional support.

The committee expresses concern about quality assurance in home tuition provided by third-party commissioning arrangements, particularly for pupils with additional needs. David Hopkins acknowledges that it is a concern when home tuition is not commissioned and managed through the local authority. Within the local authority maintained sector, if provision is not being made to meet all pupils' needs, the local authority concerned should be aware of this and deal with it.

Nick Williams emphasized the need for teacher training to include experience working in PRUs and interacting with mainstream schools. Sharon Davies highlighted the limited opportunities for networking and training among providers. Local authorities have met with health boards to discuss partnerships, including those related to supporting EOTAS learners' well-being. A general deficit in child and adolescent mental health services may be contributing to the number of learners ending up in EOTAS provisions.

New teachers lack experience dealing with challenging behaviour. Nick Williams notes that it's not just this reason for people leaving the profession. Schools have implemented policies and use a mentor to ensure consistency across the whole school, including good practice.

Concerns were raised about Dr Ian Johnson's concern regarding an increase in younger children being excluded. He noted that this trend has gone up substantially in recent years and suggested underlying factors influencing exclusions may include behavioural issues, adverse childhood experiences, and school policies. The role of accountability measures in driving exclusions was highlighted as an area for further exploration.
The discussion focused on providing adequate support for pupils at risk of becoming EOTAS. A whole-school approach was mentioned as part of the solution to address this issue.

The committee discussed addressing adverse childhood experiences in education, including teacher training and a proposed new curriculum on health and well-being. Sarah Stone emphasized the importance of young people's voices and skills in understanding emotions. Liz Williams highlighted concerns about awareness and knowledge of children with additional learning needs. Schools need to adapt their practices to support individual children, recognizing that up to 50% may have an additional learning need.

The committee discussed the importance of addressing adverse childhood experiences in various settings, particularly for vulnerable groups who have experienced early trauma. Dr Ian Johnson mentioned that his previous school had become a trauma-informed school and highlighted the need to consider the emotional and mental health well-being needs of this group. The panel emphasized the need to intervene early and prevent challenges from becoming long-term issues.

The panel discussed concerns about self-harm and mental health issues among young people, particularly noting a concern regarding how girls experience this within the school environment and in general, with increases in some cases. Support levels in schools depend strongly on senior management team commitment. Off-rolling pupils with mental health problems is a concern across various settings.

Liz Williams discussed how children with communication difficulties and anxiety issues are often labeled as "naughty" due to a lack of formal diagnosis and understanding from teachers. Dr Ian Johnson highlighted concerns about learners disclosing problems due to fear of not being believed or accepted. The Estyn report notes that there are generally fewer off-rollings from Welsh-medium education to EOTAS, with potential reasons including socio-economic profiles and availability of EOTAS provision.

The discussion centered on delays for young people not in education or training and how these delays exacerbate pre-existing inequalities. Concerns were raised about home schooling's lack of transparency, which makes vulnerable children "hidden or invisible". The panel also discussed individual tuition, including the need to support students reintroduced into mainstream education if needed.

There may be some pluses as well as minuses regarding exclusions and their impact on mental well-being. Support for excluded children is often fragmented across different institutions, with clear responsibility lying with local education authorities in this context. Parents need clear information to support their children effectively.

Dr Ian Johnson highlighted that turning schools into places that consider mental health and emotional well-being first is key to improving the well-being of future generations. He emphasized the importance of a statutory whole-school approach in foregrounding this issue within school ethos. Sarah Stone discussed the link between inequality, adverse childhood experiences, and the opportunity to intervene positively to prevent young people from falling out of school and society.

Here are the flagged sentences:

ISSUE [1]: The panel suggests that challenging behaviour, combined with additional learning needs, contributes to the rise in exclusions.
REASON: The transcript does not explicitly state that the two factors "contribute" to the rise in exclusions. Nick Williams and Sharon Davies mention challenging behavior and additional learning needs as possible reasons for the increase in exclusions, but they do not imply causation.

ISSUE [2]: Many local authorities are developing strategies for support and reintegration.
REASON: While Nick Williams mentions that "many local authorities" have developed a tiered approach to provision, he also notes that there is a lag in implementing these strategies consistently across all areas.

ISSUE [3]: School accountability measures place pressures on schools.
REASON: This sentence is accurate according to the transcript.

After reviewing the summary against the transcript section, I found some issues:

ISSUE [1]: The discussion touched on various topics including a whole-family approach to supporting families with complex needs and training for staff.
REASON: This sentence gets the fact wrong - it says "training for staff", but the transcript actually mentions "training on adverse childhood experiences" specifically in schools.

ISSUE [2]: Funding challenges, particularly due to the introduction of the new Act, are placing pressure on schools.
REASON: The transcript does not specify that funding challenges are directly due to the introduction of the new Act. It mentions the new Act as a contributing factor, but also other factors such as delegation levels and pressures on staff.

ISSUE [3]: Local authorities have acknowledged that budget constraints may lead to increased exclusions for students with additional learning needs.
REASON: The transcript actually says that David Hopkins from the WLGA mentioned delegation levels being high in most authority areas, and does not explicitly state that local authorities have acknowledged budget constraints leading to increased exclusions. However, it is implied by Sharon Davies' statement about schools looking at their funding and making hard choices.

ISSUE [4]: The discussion touched on a multi-agency approach, with agencies working together to support families.
REASON: This sentence gets the fact wrong - while the transcript does mention a multi-agency approach, it also emphasizes the importance of considering the whole-family approach, not just multiple agencies working together.

ISSUE [1]: The new curriculum is seen as an opportunity to improve practices.
REASON: The transcript states that "the new curriculum coming in" provides an opportunity for a broader range of measures, not specifically improving practices.

ISSUE [2]: Getting children back into mainstream education at key stage 4 is more difficult due to being closer to leaving statutory education.
REASON: According to Nick Williams, it's actually the younger learners (15-16) that are more challenging to reintegrate, whereas the transcript doesn't mention age as a factor for difficulty in reintegration.

REASON: The summary implies that concerns were raised against Dr Ian Johnson's concern, but the transcript shows that his concern is actually shared by others (e.g. Lynne Neagle AM, Sarah Stone).

ISSUE [2]:
He noted that this trend has gone up substantially in recent years...

REASON: This sentence states "recent years", but the transcript does not specify how long ago these trends started; it mentions an increase in exclusions "in the past few years" without a specific time frame.

NO_FURTHER_ISSUES

ISSUE [1]: The discussion focused on providing adequate support for pupils at risk of becoming EOTAS.

REASON: This summary does not reflect the complexity of the discussion. While the topic was indeed about support for EOTAS pupils, the transcript section also highlighted the importance of a whole-school approach and the potential consequences of exclusion, which is not captured in this summary sentence.

ISSUE [2]: A whole-school approach was mentioned as part of the solution to address this issue.

REASON: The transcript section does mention a whole-school approach, but it also mentions that it should be statutory provision. This detail is omitted from the summary sentence.

Here are the flagged issues:

ISSUE [1]: The committee discussed addressing adverse childhood experiences in education, including teacher training and a proposed new curriculum on health and well-being.

REASON: This summary suggests that the new curriculum is solely focused on health and well-being, whereas the transcript mentions that it will also cover mental health awareness. (In the transcript, Sarah Stone says "health and well-being and mental health and awareness".)

ISSUE [2]: Liz Williams highlighted concerns about awareness and knowledge of children with additional learning needs.

REASON: The summary implies that Liz Williams mentioned that schools are not aware of children's additional learning needs, but she actually said that participants at the round table were concerned that staff may be unaware of these needs. (In the transcript, Liz Williams says "participants said that they were particularly concerned about the lack of awareness and knowledge of children who had additional learning needs".)

ISSUE [3]: Schools need to adapt their practices to support individual children, recognizing that up to 50% may have an additional learning need.

REASON: The summary implies that this is a general statement by Liz Williams, but it's actually based on a participant's comment at the round table. (In the transcript, Liz Williams quotes a participant as saying "up to 50 per cent of learners in a classroom could have an additional learning need".)

After reviewing the summary against the transcript section, I found the following errors:

ISSUE 1:
The committee discussed the importance of addressing adverse childhood experiences in various settings...

REASON: The transcript does not mention "various settings". It focuses on schools and education in Wales.

ISSUE 2:
Dr Ian Johnson mentioned that his previous school had become a trauma-informed school and highlighted the need to consider the emotional and mental health well-being needs of this group.

REASON: Although Dr. Ian Johnson mentions his previous experience as a school governor, he does not explicitly state that his school became "trauma-informed". He says it is somewhere that has "established" being trauma-informed and are widening its base.

ISSUE 3:
The panel emphasized the need to intervene early and prevent challenges from becoming long-term issues.

REASON: The transcript section mentions intervening early, but not the specific phrase "preventing challenges from becoming long-term issues". It focuses on preventing lifelong isolation and addressing the impact of early trauma.

After carefully reviewing the summary against the transcript section, I have found some issues:

ISSUE [1]: The panel discussed concerns about self-harm and mental health issues among young people, particularly noting a concern regarding how girls experience this within the school environment and in general, with increases in some cases.

REASON: The transcript does not mention that there are "increases" in self-harm among young people in general. It mentions specific "quite substantial increases" among some groups, but it does not quantify or specify the extent of these increases for girls in general.

ISSUE [2]: Support levels in schools depend strongly on senior management team commitment.

REASON: The transcript states that Dr. Ian Johnson's experience is that support levels in schools "depend quite strongly on the senior management team and their commitment", but it does not say that this is a universal rule. It also mentions that he has seen uneven outcomes, suggesting that there are other factors at play.

ISSUE [3]: Off-rolling pupils with mental health problems is a concern across various settings.

REASON: The transcript mentions off-rolling as a concern, but it specifically states that Liz Williams suspects it might be more prevalent in EOTAS or PRU provision rather than mainstream schools. It does not mention other settings.

ISSUE [4]: Dawn Bowden AM asks about the prevalence of labeling children as "naughty" and Dr. Ian Johnson doesn't respond to this question, but Liz Williams mentions that she has come across it anecdotally in her experience working with pupil referral units.

REASON: The summary suggests that Dr. Ian Johnson responds to this question, but he does not.

After reviewing the summary against the transcript section, I have identified some factual errors:

ISSUE [1]: "Liz Williams discussed how children with communication difficulties and anxiety issues are often labeled as 'naughty' due to a lack of formal diagnosis and understanding from teachers."
REASON: The transcript actually says that children can be labelled "naughty" even if they haven't been formally diagnosed, but it is the label itself that becomes an impediment to getting support, rather than a lack of diagnosis being the cause.

ISSUE [2]: "Dr Ian Johnson highlighted concerns about learners disclosing problems due to fear of not being believed or accepted."
REASON: The transcript actually says that learners may feel uncomfortable disclosing problems because they fear not being taken seriously, rather than not being believed or accepted.

ISSUE [3]: "The Estyn report notes that there are generally fewer off-rollings from Welsh-medium education to EOTAS, with potential reasons including socio-economic profiles and availability of EOTAS provision."
REASON: The transcript actually says that the Estyn report notes that there are generally fewer off-rolling from Welsh-medium education to EOTAS, but lists a different set of potential reasons, including socio-economic profile and lack of EOTAS provision within a local area.

ISSUE [4]: "Dr Ian Johnson highlighted concerns about learners disclosing problems due to fear of not being believed or accepted."
REASON: The transcript actually says that Dr Ian Johnson mentioned a young boy's concern about being taken seriously when disclosing problems, rather than concerns about being believed or accepted.

Here are the flagged sentences:

ISSUE 1:
The discussion centered on delays for young people not in education or training and how these delays exacerbate pre-existing inequalities.

REASON: The transcript does not mention "pre-existing inequalities" as a direct phrase, but it mentions that being out of school exacerbates inequalities and disadvantage.

Here are the flagged sentences:

ISSUE 1:
There may be some pluses as well as minuses regarding exclusions and their impact on mental well-being.
REASON: The transcript mentions that there "may be some pluses as well as minuses" but does not specifically focus on this aspect, whereas the summary statement implies a balanced view.

ISSUE 2:
Support for excluded children is often fragmented across different institutions, with clear responsibility lying with local education authorities in this context.
REASON: The transcript mentions that "the responsibility lies on the local education authority in that context" (Dr. Ian Johnson), but it also mentions that there are multiple institutions involved and that a national picture of support is lacking.

ISSUE 3:
Parents need clear information to support their children effectively.
REASON: The transcript states that parents "must know where to go for support themselves, and I think that a parent can't necessarily know the rights of their child to education and what their child is supposed to be having if they're not provided with all of the information" (Liz Williams), but it does not explicitly say that parents need clear information to support their children effectively.

ISSUE 4:
...and how the whole-school approach will work within EOTAS, and how we ensure that the provision is available through the medium of Welsh...
REASON: The transcript mentions Dr. Ian Johnson's comment about the importance of providing support for vulnerable children, including those in EOTAS situations, but it does not mention anything about the provision being available through the medium of Welsh.

NO_OTHER_ISSUES

After re-reading, I found additional issues:

ISSUE 5:
Lynne Neagle AM: We've got some questions now from Siân Gwenllian.
REASON: The transcript states "Sian Gwenllian AM", not "Siân Gwenllian".

Let me know if you would like me to clarify or correct anything.

ISSUE [1]:
Dr Ian Johnson highlighted that turning schools into places that consider mental health and emotional well-being first is key to improving the well-being of future generations.
REASON: The transcript says "considering mental health and emotional well-being first" rather than "places that consider", which implies a physical transformation rather than an attitude shift.

ISSUE [2]:
He emphasized the importance of a statutory whole-school approach in foregrounding this issue within school ethos.
REASON: Lynne Neagle AM is the one who mentions Item 4, and it's about papers to note, not Dr Ian Johnson's emphasis on a statutory whole-school approach.

ISSUE [3]:
Sarah Stone discussed the link between inequality, adverse childhood experiences, and the opportunity to intervene positively to prevent young people from falling out of school and society.
REASON: The transcript says "out of sight" rather than "society", which implies a more literal sense of being out of view rather than abandoning or rejecting societal norms.
chunk_summaries_after: The panel suggests that challenging behaviour and additional learning needs may be contributing factors to the rise in exclusions. Local authorities are developing strategies for support and reintegration, but there is a lag in implementing them consistently across all areas due to insufficient capacity. School accountability measures place pressures on schools.

The discussion highlighted the importance of a whole-family approach in supporting families with complex needs. Training on adverse childhood experiences and trauma-informed practice has been provided to schools. Funding challenges are placing pressure on schools, particularly due to high delegation levels and staff pressures. The introduction of the new Act may exacerbate these pressures.

Variation in provision between terms and weeks creates challenges for retaining pupils in mainstream schools, potentially leading to exclusions. The new curriculum coming in provides an opportunity to agree on a broader range of measures. Getting children back into mainstream education at key stage 4 is more challenging due to the type of learners involved.

A model for retaining pupils in school settings has been tried elsewhere, including Oxfordshire, where a local authority looked at partnerships within schools to keep money in schools and prevent learners being moved around. Capacity issues are caused by not having enough spaces in some cases. Nick Williams said safeguarding is at the forefront of their thinking, but providing additional training to staff in EOTAS provision is a challenge.

Quality assurance of alternative provision can be challenging due to difficulties in accessing home settings. Providers may use their own staff for alternative provision, which is seen as a way to address concerns about quality assurance. The WLGA paper expresses concerns about changes to pupil registration and their implications for the management of data/funding. There are efforts to move away from off-rolling of students, focusing on supporting vulnerable learners.

The committee expresses concern about inconsistency in provision of education for pupils with additional needs outside of PRUs and local authority commissioned tuition. David Hopkins acknowledges that it is a concern when home tuition is provided by third-party commissioning arrangements. Within the local authority maintained sector, if provision is not being made to meet all pupils' needs, the issue may arise.

Nick Williams emphasized the need for teacher training to include experience working in PRUs. Sharon Davies highlighted the limited opportunities for networking and training among providers. Nick Williams mentioned that local authorities have met with health boards to discuss partnerships. A general deficit in child and adolescent mental health services may be contributing to more pupils having additional needs, which could translate into those ending up in EOTAS provisions.

New teachers lack experience dealing with challenging behaviour. Nick Williams notes that it's not just this reason for people leaving the profession. Schools use a mentor to ensure consistency across the whole school, and policies are in place to promote good practice. Janet Finch-Saunders raises concerns about transport issues for EOTAS learners in rural areas, citing large distances as a problem.

Concerns were raised about an increase in younger children being excluded, which has gone up substantially in the past few years. Dr Ian Johnson noted that underlying factors influencing exclusions may include behavioural issues and adverse childhood experiences. The role of accountability measures in driving exclusions was highlighted as an area for further exploration.

The discussion highlighted the importance of a whole-school approach in supporting pupils at risk of becoming EOTAS, which should be statutory provision. This approach aims to prevent exclusion and address adverse childhood experiences by understanding the child's individual needs within the school environment, with consequences for exclusion including an elevated risk of suicide if not addressed.

The committee discussed addressing adverse childhood experiences in education, including teacher training and a proposed new curriculum on health, well-being, mental health, and awareness. Sarah Stone emphasized the importance of young people's voices and skills in understanding emotions. Liz Williams highlighted concerns about participants' lack of awareness and knowledge of children with additional learning needs, noting that up to 50% may have these needs.

The committee discussed the importance of addressing adverse childhood experiences in schools and education in Wales, particularly for vulnerable groups who have experienced early trauma. Dr Ian Johnson mentioned his previous experience as a school governor where he saw the establishment of a trauma-informed approach being widened. The panel emphasized intervening early to prevent lifelong isolation and address the impact of early trauma on individuals' mental health and well-being.

The panel discussed concerns about self-harm and mental health issues among young people, particularly how girls experience this within the school environment and in general, noting quite substantial increases in some cases. Support levels in schools depend on the senior management team's commitment. Off-rolling pupils with mental health problems is a concern, potentially more prevalent in EOTAS or PRU provision.

Liz Williams discussed how children with communication difficulties and anxiety issues are often labelled "naughty" due to a lack of understanding from teachers. The label itself becomes an impediment to getting support. Dr Ian Johnson highlighted concerns about learners feeling uncomfortable disclosing problems due to fear of not being taken seriously. The Estyn report notes that there are generally fewer off-rolling from Welsh-medium education to EOTAS, potentially due to socio-economic profiles and lack of EOTAS provision within a local area.

The discussion centered on delays for young people not in education or training and how these delays exacerbate inequalities and disadvantage. Concerns were raised about home schooling's lack of transparency, which makes vulnerable children "hidden or invisible". The panel also discussed individual tuition, including the need to support students reintroduced into mainstream education if needed.

Support for excluded children is often fragmented across different institutions. The responsibility lies with local education authorities in this context. Parents must know where to go for support themselves and need clear information about their child's rights and entitlements. A national picture of support for these children is lacking.

Dr Ian Johnson emphasized the importance of a statutory whole-school approach in foregrounding mental health and emotional well-being within school ethos. He highlighted that considering mental health and emotional well-being first is key to improving the well-being of future generations. Sarah Stone discussed the link between inequality, adverse childhood experiences, and the opportunity to intervene positively to prevent young people from falling out of school and being socially abandoned.

education_9.transcript: Lynne Neagle AM: Good morning, everyone. Welcome to the Children, Young People and Education Committee this morning. I've received no apologies for absence. Suzy Davies is going to be arriving a little bit late. Are there any declarations of interest, please? No? Okay. Item 2, then, this morning is an evidence session to scrutinise the Welsh Government's progress in developing the new curriculum for Wales. I'm very pleased to welcome Kirsty Williams AM, Minister for Education; Steve Davies, director of the education directorate; and Claire Bennett, who is deputy director for curriculum and assessment. Thank you all for attending this morning, and thank you for the paper that you provided in advance to the committee. We've got a lot of ground to cover, so if it's okay, we'll go straight into questions. If I can just start by asking you what the main messages are that you've received during the feedback period on the draft curriculum. Kirsty Williams AM: Thank you, Chair. If I may, I think it's important to understand what the level of that feedback has been. So, there was a concerted effort and a plan drawn up to try and ensure that there was as much engagement, knowledge and opportunity as possible. So, working through the regional consortia, approximately 120 separate events were organised, and we believe that in the region of 6,000 headteachers, teachers, governors and teaching assistants have actually had an opportunity to participate in those events. We also held a number of focus group sessions specifically for young people themselves, so that they could give us their feedback. There were 24 focus groups that were arranged for children and young people. We also held some specific events to engage with the business community. We'll all be familiar, won't we, with the narrative of, 'Oh, we're sending people out into the world of business without the skills that we as an employer are looking for'. So we thought it was really important to engage business so that they can have their say and their input into the process. And obviously we work very hard on making it as easy as possible via new technology for people to have their say. So, Members, I'm sure, will have been aware of the specific pages on Hwb that outlined the draft curriculum, and we had 275,000 unique visits to the Hwb curriculum pages. It's really interesting to see the breakdown of the areas of learning and experience—which particular AoLEs were the most popular and were being looked at the most—with our creative and performing arts and the creativity and the expressive arts being the most popular. So that's really interesting that people really wanted to engage in the content of that particular AoLE. What, then, have people said to us? Well, I'm really pleased that there has been broad support for the curriculum changes that we are proposing. There's real support for the need for change, because that's the first question, actually; why are we doing this, and why do we need to change? So, support for the need for change, and lots of support for the principle of a purpose-led curriculum. So, good levels of support for the four purposes, and that driving the content below it. Strong support for greater autonomy and agency for the profession—so, the ability of the profession to take a framework and then truly let them adapt it to meet the needs of the children that they are working with in their communities. Also, a great welcome of the emphasis on formative assessment and the importance of formative assessment. In terms of some of the things that people are asking us to look again at, some of that is around some of the language used. Can we clarify, can we simplify in some areas, are there things that are repeated in a variety of AoLEs? Can we use that as an overarching, rather than repeating ourselves? Can we simplify it and clarify some of the language? Also, in some areas—. It's interesting; in some areas people want it simplified and cut down, but in other areas, people say, 'Well actually, in this bit of it, we need a bit more detail, and a bit more depth and clarity'. So we'll be reflecting on all the feedback that we've had. That process has already started, but considering that this is a massive change, I have been hugely encouraged, actually, by the high levels of engagement and support for the broad principles of what we're doing. Lynne Neagle AM: Okay. Thank you, Minister. You said that you're reflecting on the messages that you've received. Are you able to give the committee any early indication of what level of change you anticipate making to the guidance? Kirsty Williams AM: I think what's really important and what has been the strength of the process to date is that we are not doing this to our profession, we are doing it with our profession. So, in the spirit of co-construction, the reflection on the feedback will continue to be primarily led by the existing infrastructure that we already had that got us to this stage at present. So, Members will be aware that we've slightly changed the model. We had our pioneer school model and we have slightly refined that now. Pioneers were asked if they wanted to continue in that process and to put themselves forward, and we've narrowed that down now to a smaller group of innovative schools. But above and beyond the innovative schools, we looked at individuals who have specific expertise in subject areas, and they're the first part of that process. So, they met last week to begin looking at the feedback, and we'll continue to use the processes that we have to reflect and refine. Are there big changes to the concept? No. Opportunities to do things better, explain things better, simplify where possible, where we've been told that that needs to happen, provide greater depth where we've been told that needs to happen—absolutely. We're definitely very alive and very willing to engage in those, but in terms of the overall concept, then no, no significant changes. Lynne Neagle AM: Okay. Thank you. And what are the steps now before the final curriculum is published in January 2020? Kirsty Williams AM: Okay. So, the quality—. We call them—it's not a very nice name—quality improvement practitioners, the QI practitioners. So, these are the subject specialists. They met last week to begin this process. In October, there will be a number of workshops lasting three days at a time where those practitioners will continue that process of feedback with our curriculum and assessment group and all those people involved. By November, we would expect the QI groups to have completed their work and would want them to be in a position to hand over the refinements to an editorial process, and that has to be done in both languages. I think it's really important that we don't do it in English and then we simply translate it into Welsh. So, the editorial process will then be engaged to draw up the final example. It also, then, hands over to the publication team to do all the work on the publication, our website team will then be working on it, and then we would expect final publication in January—am I right, Claire? Claire Bennett: Yes. Kirsty Williams AM: Yes, January. Lynne Neagle AM: Okay. Thank you. We've got some specific questions now on the legislation from Siân Gwenllian. Sian Gwenllian AM: You've touched on the point that there's going to be some sort of change, and you've mentioned in your paper to us that further specific aspects of the framework of the curriculum are going to be included in the primary legislation. We want to know what those aspects will be and what has made you change your mind. Kirsty Williams AM: The original proposal that we began working on was, in the legislation, to provide for the four purposes—so, the four purposes would be set out in the legislation—as well as putting in law the areas of learning and experience that you'll all be familiar with. Then, below that, we were going to legislate for a number of the cross-curricular aspects—so, the literacy, the numeracy and the digital competency—as well as some elements where we had already said that we were going to make that statutory, so, for instance, above and beyond what Graham Donaldson would have put in his original reports. A good example of that is relationships and sexuality education. I've already made an announcement that I was going to put RSE on the face of the Bill. So, the two main new areas that we are now working on to include within the Bill are to ensure that there is breadth within the curriculum for everybody—. So, we will make a statutory provision for the 'what matters' statements. So, we're bringing it a step lower again. Rather than simply, in law, leaving it at the AoLE level, we'll be bringing it down again to the 'what matters' statements within that, again, providing greater certainty and greater clarity about our expectations at a national level. We'll also be looking to include in the legislation provision for a statutory framework setting out our approach to progression in each of the AoLE areas. So, there has been in the Chamber—I can see Suzy is writing this down—Suzy has asked me questions about, 'How do you create a national expectation around progression?' We've reflected on that and our conversations with other people outside during this process, so we would look to have a statutory framework where our expectations of progression at a national level would be laid out. As I said, we are proposing those changes because of responses that we've had. One of the consistent worries that some people have had, whilst being very supportive of the overall aims, is how do you get that balance between individual autonomy in the school, but also ensure that there is some national expectation and that the variation on these important things is not so great as to cause concern. So, we've reflected on that. I set up a process—this was an open process, and we were open to listening to people. So, I think those will be the two main areas where we hadn't originally thought that we would legislate for, but that we will now legislate for. Sian Gwenllian AM: Okay. Great. That's very helpful. I haven't got the 'what matters' statements in front of me— Kirsty Williams AM: Well, if you remember, we have the four purposes, and then we have the areas of learning and experience, and then, below the areas of learning and experience are the broad concepts that we would expect to be delivered in each of those areas of learning and experience. So, we're going to be refining some of those. Some of the feedback that we've had is that— Sian Gwenllian AM: So, will you be adding to those? Kirsty Williams AM: Adding is not necessarily—no, not adding. But, for instance, the children have given us some interesting feedback about what they feel really matters in those areas of learning and experience. So, they'll be refined, but not added to, and then we will legislate for them, and that hadn't been the original intention. Sian Gwenllian AM: Okay. Will they include mental health and well-being? Kirsty Williams AM: Well, the area of learning and experience for well-being is already there, and underneath that area of learning title, there are the broad concepts of what matters, what we believe matters, in that area of learning, and it is that that we will now actually put into the legislation. Sorry, I'm not helping, because I haven't got them with me either to read them out. Lynne Neagle AM: They are in the annex to the paper, Siân, and, obviously, mental health is in there. So, just to clarify, then, that would mean that every school would have to teach mental health by law. Kirsty Williams AM: Yes, because the 'what matters' states very clearly the broad concepts in health and well-being. It refers to both physical and mental health, and we intend to legislate to ensure that the 'what matters' statements are a set given in the system. Lynne Neagle AM: Thank you. Kirsty Williams AM: Claire, am I explaining it okay? Claire Bennett: Yes, the 'what matters' are the articulation of the big ideas. These are the essence of the core content. So, the idea is to make sure that those are consistent, and then that still leaves huge flexibility underneath as to how to approach those, which particular topics to select in how to actually teach them. But the concept that you might not do one 'what matters'—it was never what was intended. They were always intended to represent, as a whole, the learning that every learner should get. They won't be literally in the Bill, because, obviously, you might want to change the emphasis, so that'll be provided for in subordinate legislation, but the provision will be there, and they will have the status of something that's not optional, basically, for a school. Sian Gwenllian AM: Therefore, it's not going to be on the face of the Bill—the mental health aspect, for example. And there are other matters that we've raised here. This is what I'm not clear about. You're saying that it's subordinate, but then you're saying— Kirsty Williams AM: So, on the face of the Bill, we will make provision to say that the 'what matters' statements have to be delivered. Over time, the 'what matters' statements might change. So the actual wording of the 'what matters' statements will be in secondary legislation; the need to deliver and the requirement, the legal requirement to deliver the 'what matters' statement, will be on the face of the Bill. So, for instance, education is changing all the time. If I think, if we had sat here 20 years ago, we probably, in a 'what matters' statement on health and well-being, wouldn't have referred to mental health, 20 years ago, because our understanding as a society, our willingness as a society to engage in that—. So, if we had drawn up a 'what matters' statement even a decade ago, I suspect we wouldn't have talked about mental health. So, the concept of having to deliver the 'what matters' statement will be in the primary legislation; the actual wording, because otherwise if you wanted to change it you'd have to go through the entire process—. So, the wording of the 'what matters' statement will be in secondary legislation. The actual need and the compulsion, the expectation that you have to do that, will be on the face of the Bill. Lynne Neagle AM: Suzy, you had a supplementary. Suzy Davies AM: Just on this question of the distinction between primary and secondary legislation. The point you've made is one that Government has made with other legislation prior to this, but can I just ask you to consider the worthiness, if you like, or the good purpose of actually putting the wording of the 'what matters' statements, the first round of those, in the primary legislation on the basis that they can be amended through affirmative procedure secondary legislation when they need changing in due course? The reason I ask this is just to explain to the population of Wales that there is certainty at the first step, bearing in mind that it will change over the years—I completely accept that. But when you're amending primary legislation, you don't have to go through the whole process again—you can do it via secondary legislation provided the correct powers are put in the primary legislation to do that. So, I'm just asking you to consider that. Kirsty Williams AM: Yes, as I said, I think we've demonstrated that we're listening to people, that there has been concern expressed about certainty and having a national approach on some of these issues, and we have taken steps to address that and we'll continue to reflect, but, crucially, we'll continue to reflect with our partners who are co-constructing this with us. And I think the important thing to remember is that it's not Ministers or civil servants that are necessarily drawing up these 'what matters' statements, it is practitioners themselves, guided by experts in the field that are not teachers, that have come up with these things. Lynne Neagle AM: Okay, thank you. Obviously, the Bill will presumably come to this committee— Kirsty Williams AM: We're assuming so. Lynne Neagle AM: And we'll have an opportunity to influence it. Siân. Sian Gwenllian AM: Moving on, therefore, to the religious education and relationships and sexuality education, I understand that you've had numerous responses to the White Paper surrounding this particular area. What are the main points that were raised with you and how do you intend to respond to what's been said? Kirsty Williams AM: Well, these two areas certainly have ensured that lots of people have responded. It's interesting that people are far more interested in what we may or may not do about these two subjects than maths, English, Welsh, science, but there we go; I guess it's the nature of the areas that we're talking about. With regard to religious education, we had a significant number of people that have expressed concerns about our approaches towards RE that were set out in the White Paper. I think amongst the things that people have objected to were—. There were very mixed views on the inclusion of a range of faiths and world religions included in that area of learning. Many people said that there needed to be a much clearer and stronger—and in some cases exclusive—focus on Christianity, as opposed to including other world religions and, indeed, non-religious views. So, secular views or spiritual—spirituality rather than organised religion. There were people who thought that RE shouldn't be compulsory at all and therefore our proposals to ensure that RE was compulsory, people objected to that, on the other—. And there were many responses that were concerned about and emphasised the need to respect parents' views. So, if a child's parents have certain views, those are to be respected. Of those respondents that agreed that RE should be a compulsory part of the curriculum, or were neutral—didn't express an opinion either way, but were neutral on the question—the issues that they were bringing forward were: a need for learners, as they saw it, to be prepared to be part of a diverse and multicultural society. So, they wanted RE to be much more broad-based and encompassing of world views and world religions. They felt that that was an important part of preparing a young person to live in a world that is, as you say, diverse with people of different views living in it. There is certainly a need to modernise RE; some people perceived the current curriculum as a bit old-fashioned. And also there was much feedback on making sure that the profession was ready to deliver a renewed, modernised RE curriculum. So, those are the issues around religious education— Sian Gwenllian AM: Can I just—? Before you go on to the next section—is there evidence that this was a co-ordinated lobby to present a particular view and what is your response going to be to this? Obviously, there are always going to be lobbies presenting particular viewpoints. Our role, as politicians, is to lead, obviously, isn't it? Kirsty Williams AM: Yes. Claire, would you say that it was a co-ordinated response? Claire Bennett: It wasn't a campaign in the sense of it being completely consistent, but, certainly, I think people with a particular interest in this issues felt galvanised to respond to the White Paper on this issue—on this one and on relationships and sex education. Sian Gwenllian AM: Okay. And your response? Kirsty Williams AM: And my response: well, clearly, Siân, I need to consider those responses, and both for RE and RSE, I shall be making a statement in the near future of our intentions on how to respond to these issues. With regard to RSE, the key messages, again, are focused on whether children should be taught RSE at all and that this should not be in the curriculum and it shouldn't be a compulsory part of the curriculum—that this was not an area where the school system and the education system should be involved, and that it should be alone the preserve of parents to teach children about issues around relationships and sexuality education. There were some issues raised about potential challenges with staff in talking about issues that they perhaps personally did not agree with with regard to this curriculum. So, those were the main areas that people were concerned about. With regard to guaranteed access to a full curriculum, there were strong views that, again, it should be parents, and parents alone, that made decisions about whether their children should have access to the entirety of the curriculum rather than schools or the state setting those rules. So, I have to reflect on what has come back as part of the consultation exercise. I think these are really important aspects of our curriculum. I think if we are to achieve the four purposes, and that's how we have always got to think about it, and if we agree that those are the kind of people and individuals we want leaving our compulsory education system—how do we achieve those purposes? How are they healthy, confident individuals? How are we to prepare our children to be ethical, informed citizens of Wales and the world? So, we will reflect on what has come back to us, but I do believe that these are important aspects of the curriculum, if we are to achieve the four purposes. Sian Gwenllian AM: Okay, thank you. You've also mentioned that there will be a final draft of the curriculum at the start of next year. What is the timetable for the Bill itself and for seeking Royal Assent for that? Kirsty Williams AM: Okay, so, as I said earlier, the expectation is that we will publish a final version of the curriculum in January 2020, so schools will then have the opportunity to be really engaging in it. I have to say, I'm in schools most weeks, and many, many, many schools are already taking the opportunity, even on the draft, to begin to think about planning and, indeed, changing what they're doing in schools. I'm overwhelmed, actually, by the enthusiasm of the sector to embrace what is a massive change for them. So, that's January. We would expect to introduce the Bill following the Easter recess. Sian Gwenllian AM: Okay. That gives enough time, then, for it to be— Kirsty Williams AM: Yes. Sian Gwenllian AM: —actually implemented in September— Kirsty Williams AM: In 2022. Sian Gwenllian AM: In 2022, yes. Kirsty Williams AM: And I think what's really important is that we get the actual curriculum out in January itself, because that's the bit that schools are really concerned about, and then we will have the process here to underpin it. Lynne Neagle AM: Okay, thank you very much. We've got some questions now on potential unintended consequences, and other matters relating to that, from Hefin David. Hefin David AM: Can I ask what value you place on the work done by the Wales Institute of Social and Economic Research, Data and Methods and the presentation so far and the paper to be presented by Dr Nigel Newton? Kirsty Williams AM: I always—and Welsh Government are big supporters of WISERD and the work that they do, and, in fact, we need more research into Welsh education, not less. Therefore, I find their papers really helpful and help us to inform our work. I think what's really important in perhaps this piece of work is to say that I hope that, in the time where I've had an influence to influence over Welsh education, either as a backbencher or now as a Minister, equality and principles of equity have always driven what I've tried to achieve. And I would never undertake a policy reform that I thought would lead to less equity in the Welsh education system. Closing the attainment gap is a core element of the national mission for education in Wales and we would not proceed with anything that we thought could lead to an exacerbation of an achievement gap. Hefin David AM: Okay. And how do you respond, then—? I mean, it's a balanced paper, it looks at pros and cons and there's a mixed picture from it. How do you respond to the specific statement that Dr Nigel Newton said that the curriculum could exacerbate segregation within schools between different groups of pupils? Kirsty Williams AM: Well, I think what the paper acknowledges is that there is no evidence that that will happen. These are 'coulds' and 'maybes' and things that we need, as a Government, to take into consideration as we plan this journey. There can't be any empirical research done at the moment because the curriculum isn't being delivered, but I understand, and we need listen to—. If there are concerns out there in the field that these are unintended consequences that we may fall into—that is the value of that piece of research that helps inform us. I have to say, though, the curriculum in itself is neither going to necessarily on its own hugely enhance equity nor detract from equity, in the sense that the curriculum is what's taught in our schools. There is an opportunity, I believe, that empowering teachers to be able to be more flexible in what they teach their children actually gives us an opportunity to deliver lessons that could be much more engaging and much more relevant to some of our schoolchildren than what they have at the moment. What will make the curriculum a success for all of our children, and I believe will have a bigger impact on children who are in danger of being left behind, are the four enabling purposes of the curriculum. So, the curriculum on its own can play a part, but it will only be as good as the four enabling elements that surround it. And that is strong leadership of our schools that ensures that there is no segregation, that has high expectation of all of our children, and delivers a curriculum within that setting that meets the needs of the children there. Secondly: excellent teaching. In the end, no education system, whatever its curriculum, can exceed the quality of the people who stand in front of our children day in, day out. So, the curriculum can be the most exciting, wonderful—and I think it is exciting and I think it's wonderful—it can be the most exciting, wonderful thing in the world, but if teachers can't teach it effectively, if their pedagogy is not excellent, then the content itself—it won't work. Then we've got issues around assessment and accountability. So, how do you assess how children are doing in your new curriculum? How do you understand how that pupil, who has, you know—who could be vulnerable for a whole host of reasons, usually reasons outside of the school—? That pupil is vulnerable. How can you assess how that pupil is and move their learning along in an appropriate fashion? And then, finally, the well-being of the child. People sometimes say, 'Oh, you're going soft—typical, going soft'. But what we know is that we cannot expect children to learn unless we address issues around their well-being. But we also know that poor achievement is also detrimental to a child's well-being. There's been lots and lots of research done, not in a Welsh context but in other systems, where children are kept behind for a year. That has a massive impact on their well-being. So, good achievement leads to good well-being, but good well-being also leads to good achievement, and you can't separate the two. So, the curriculum on its own will not be enough. It is the four enabling objectives that sit around it, and we have to be cognisant of the WISERD's research, of course we do, to ensure that, as we're doing our professional learning, as we're planning well-being for our children, as we think about assessment methods and how we develop a culture of strong leaders in our schools—and we have some, we have many, but we need to do more to support them—it is that that will make the biggest difference, not just the content of the curriculum on its own. Although I do believe the flexibility that we're allowing people will, I think, lead to a curriculum and more meaningful lessons for some children in schools who are in danger of disengaging because they don't understand why they're being asked to learn what they're learning, they don't see the relevance of what they're learning to what they may want to do or how their lives are, or they don't see themselves reflected. So, for some of our communities, they don't see themselves reflected in the curriculum that we're teaching at the moment. And, again, international research would suggest that, if you want a child to thrive, they have to see themselves and their community reflected in what they're learning in schools. Hefin David AM: What the WISERD research suggests is that the senior management teams—the management teams—would certainly buy into what you've just said, but the classroom teachers would be a little bit more sceptical.
